Option A confirmed: eject decomposes sealed chunks back into a record stream and feeds them through the standard routing path. This is not a performance tradeoff — it's the general case:

- Cloud vault migrations (S3, Azure) already decompose via CursorIterator → ImportRecords
- Cross-node transfers already stream records over RPC
- The O(1 filesystem rename only applies to file→file same-mount, which is the exception

This also establishes shared infrastructure for rechunking (gastrolog-3c0a6), which needs the same decompose → re-seal primitive. gastrolog-3c0a6 now depends on this issue.

### Eject action flow

1. Retention sweep matches chunk via policy (same as today)
2. Eject action opens a Cursor on the sealed chunk
3. Records stream through the route's FilterSet for per-record matching
4. Fan-out to destination vaults via standard ingest path (local append or remote forward)
5. Source chunk deleted after all records delivered

### Open questions

- Should ejected records go through the digest pipeline (re-enrichment) or bypass it?
- Backpressure: retention sweep is background, but dumping a 256MB chunk into the ingest channel could starve live ingestion. Dedicated channel or rate limiting?
- Ack semantics: live ingestion is fire-and-forget for remote vaults (RecordForwarder drops on overflow). Eject needs reliable delivery — source chunk can't be deleted until all records are confirmed delivered.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
## Design decision: record-level decomposition, not chunk-level routing

### Options considered

**A. Decompose chunks into records at eject time** — open cursor on sealed chunk, stream records through the standard routing path (FilterSet → fan-out → destination vaults). Same flow as live ingestion, just sourced from a sealed chunk instead of an ingester.

**B. Teach the router to handle chunks natively** — add chunk-level filter matching (e.g. MatchChunk on time range or source metadata), move/copy whole chunks to destinations. Preserves sealed chunk structure but can't do per-record filtering — a chunk contains mixed records with varying attributes. Would need per-chunk attribute summaries to be useful, adding significant complexity.

**C. Hybrid** — chunk-level move when route filters are trivial (catch-all), record decomposition when filters need per-record evaluation. Two code paths with different semantics for what's conceptually the same operation.

### Decision: Option A

The decompress → iterate → recompress cycle is already the general case, not an exception:
- Cloud vault migrations (S3, Azure) already decompose via CursorIterator → ImportRecords
- Cross-node transfers already stream records over RPC
- The O(1 filesystem rename only applies to file→file same-mount

This also establishes shared infrastructure for rechunking (gastrolog-3c0a6), which needs the same decompose → re-seal primitive. gastrolog-3c0a6 now depends on this issue.

Supersedes the earlier comment (gastrolog-1f3jp-c1).

### Eject action flow

1. Retention sweep matches chunk via policy (same as today)
2. Eject action opens a Cursor on the sealed chunk
3. Records stream through the route's FilterSet for per-record matching
4. Fan-out to destination vaults via standard ingest path (local append or remote forward)
5. Source chunk deleted after all records delivered

### Open questions

- Should ejected records go through the digest pipeline (re-enrichment) or bypass it?
- Backpressure: retention sweep is background, but dumping a 256MB chunk into the ingest channel could starve live ingestion. Dedicated channel or rate limiting?
- Ack semantics: live ingestion is fire-and-forget for remote vaults (RecordForwarder drops on overflow). Eject needs reliable delivery — source chunk can't be deleted until all records are confirmed delivered.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
## Answers to open questions

### 1. Digest pipeline: bypass
Ejected records have already been digested during initial ingestion. The eject path feeds directly into the routing stage (ingest()), skipping the digest loop entirely.

### 2. Backpressure: dedicated channel with priority select
A secondary `ejectCh` channel feeds into the write loop alongside the existing `digestedCh`. The write loop uses a nested select to prioritize live ingestion:

```go
for {
    select {
    case rec := <-digestedCh:
        o.ingest(rec)
        continue
    default:
    }
    select {
    case rec := <-digestedCh:
        o.ingest(rec)
    case rec := <-ejectCh:
        o.ingest(rec)
    case <-ctx.Done():
        return
    }
}
```

Live records always drain first. Ejected records only process when the live channel is empty. Starvation of eject under constant load is acceptable — the sealed chunk cursor can wait indefinitely. Fairness ratio (e.g. 100 live : 1 ejected) deferred unless starvation is observed in practice.

### 3. Delivery guarantees: still open
RecordForwarder is fire-and-forget (drops on overflow). Eject can't delete the source chunk until all records are confirmed. Needs synchronous delivery or at minimum an ack mechanism before source deletion.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
## Delivery guarantees: resolved

### Local destinations
appendLocal() writes directly to the chunk manager under the orchestrator lock. Success is synchronous — no ack needed.

### Remote destinations
Use RemoteTransferrer (synchronous, reliable) instead of RecordForwarder (async, drops on overflow). RemoteTransferrer already exists for cross-node drain transfers and provides exactly the semantics needed: caller blocks until the remote node confirms receipt.

### Source chunk deletion
Source chunk is only deleted after the full cursor has been drained and all destinations (local and remote) have confirmed delivery. If any delivery fails mid-stream, the eject aborts and the chunk remains intact. The next retention sweep picks it up again — idempotent by nature.

---

All three open questions are now resolved. Design is complete.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
## Design correction: explicit route targeting, not implicit routing

### The flaw
If ejected records flow through the same FilterSet as live ingestion, they re-match the same routes that originally stored them — creating loops or duplicating data back to the source vault. Additionally, routes intended for eject destinations would also capture live ingested records.

### The fix
The eject action explicitly names the target route(s). No FilterSet evaluation for route selection — the retention rule says "eject through route X" (or routes X, Y, Z for fan-out). The named routes then handle filtering and destination selection for the ejected records.

This mirrors the current migrate action which names a destination vault, except now it names route(s) instead. The routes themselves are regular routes — they don't need special scoping or tagging. Whether live ingestion also uses them is entirely up to the operator's wiring.

### Updated eject flow
1. Retention sweep matches chunk via policy
2. Eject action opens cursor on sealed chunk
3. Records are fed through the **explicitly named route(s)** only — not the full FilterSet
4. Each named route applies its own filter expression and fans out to its destination vaults
5. Source chunk deleted after confirmed delivery

This replaces the earlier "dump into the router" design from c2/c3.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
## Route scoping: ingestion flag

Routes get a boolean `ingestion` flag, default true. This preserves current behavior — all existing routes participate in live ingestion.

- **ingestion=true**: included in the live FilterSet, cannot be selected as an eject target
- **ingestion=false**: excluded from the live FilterSet, eligible as an eject target

When configuring a vault's eject action, the UI only offers non-ingestion routes in the route picker. Backend validation enforces the same constraint.

This keeps the model simple: a route is either for live ingestion or for eject, never both. No mixed semantics, no accidental loops.

Supersedes the "no scoping needed" claim in c5.

title: Search and Explain RPCs ignore vault_id filter for remote vaults
description:
When a query includes `vault_id=<uuid>`, the local Explain path correctly uses `ExtractVaultFilter()` to narrow to selected vaults. However, `collectRemoteExplain()` in `query.go` calls `remoteVaultsByNode()` with no filtering, fanning out ForwardExplain requests to ALL remote vaults.

Result: the execution plan shows chunks from vaults the user didn't ask for.

**Fix**: extract vault predicates from `q.BoolExpr` in `collectRemoteExplain()` and filter the remote vault list before fanning out, same as the local path does.

**Files**: `backend/internal/server/query.go` (collectRemoteExplain, ~line 1018-1042)

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: Confirmed: Search has the same bug. Both collectRemote() and collectRemoteExplain() call remoteVaultsByNode() without filtering by vault_id. The query engine on the remote node receives the full vault list and apparently doesn't apply the vault_id predicate either — all remote vaults get searched. The sidebar shows hit counts across multiple vaults despite vault_id being explicitly set in the query.

title: Config mutations must return updated config for instant UI updates
description:
Currently, config mutations (PutVault, DeleteVault, PutRotationPolicy, PutRetentionPolicy, PutIngester, PutRoute, etc.) return empty responses. The frontend relies on invalidateQueries + refetch to update the UI. In a multi-node cluster, this refetch often hits a follower whose FSM hasn't applied the Raft log yet, returning stale data cached for up to 60 seconds (staleTime: 60_000).

**Fix**: Every config mutation response includes the full `GetConfigResponse`. The response comes from the Raft leader (which has already applied the change), eliminating the stale-follower race. Frontend uses `qc.setQueryData(["config"], response.config)` for instant cache updates — no refetch, no polling, no race.

**Changes required**:
1. Proto: Add `GetConfigResponse config = N` field to all config mutation responses
2. Backend: Each handler reads config after Raft commit and includes it in the response
3. Frontend: Mutation hooks use `setQueryData` in `onSuccess` instead of `invalidateQueries`
4. Lower `staleTime` from 60s to 5s as a safety net (WatchConfig remains for cross-session updates)

**Affected RPCs**: PutVault, DeleteVault, PauseVault, ResumeVault, PutRotationPolicy, DeleteRotationPolicy, PutRetentionPolicy, DeleteRetentionPolicy, PutIngester, DeleteIngester, PutRoute, DeleteRoute, PutNodeConfig

title: Cloud vault cursor does not support Seek
description: Searching a cloud (S3) vault fails with '[internal] cloud cursor does not support Seek'. The search returns 0 results. Likely the cloud chunk cursor is missing a Seek implementation that file/memory cursors have.

title: Cross-link settings and inspector views for entities
description: When viewing an entity (vault, ingester, node, etc.) in its settings panel, there should be a link/button to jump directly to the inspector view for that entity — and vice versa, the inspector should link back to the entity's settings. Currently there's no way to navigate between the two views without manually switching tabs and finding the entity again.

title: Replace timer-based config suppression with deterministic cache invalidation
description:
The current WatchConfig → config query interaction uses a counter + 10s timeout (beginConfigSuppression / isConfigSuppressed) to prevent stale Raft follower reads from overwriting authoritative mutation responses. This works but is fragile — it relies on timing assumptions (follower lag < 10s, staleTime < suppression window) and silently drops legitimate push-updates during the suppression window.

Promising approach: version/generation counter. Mutations return a config version; WatchConfig messages carry the version that triggered them. Only invalidate if the stream version > the cache version.

The goal is zero-timer, zero-race config updates — mutations are immediately authoritative, and push-updates never regress the cache.

title: Timestamp field equality matching broken by precision mismatch
description: Event identity search fails because ingest_ts equality comparison uses string matching between Go RFC3339Nano (nanosecond precision) and JavaScript toISOString (millisecond precision). The strings never match, so the query returns no results. Fix: parse both sides as time.Time and truncate to the filter precision before comparing.

title: Migrate frontend timestamp handling to Temporal API
description: Replace Date-based timestamp handling with the Temporal API for nanosecond precision. Currently using a manual formatTimestampNano helper to work around Date's millisecond truncation. Temporal.Instant natively supports nanoseconds and would eliminate this class of bug. User has prior experience with the Temporal polyfill.

title: bug: Test Connection fails on running listener ingesters
description:
TestConnection on listener ingesters (syslog, relp, http, otlp, fluentfwd) does a trial bind (net.Listen then close) to check if the port is available. This always fails with "address already in use" when the ingester is already running, because the ingester itself holds the port.

The test was designed to validate config before starting, not to health-check a running ingester. It should detect that the ingester is already active and either skip the bind check or report the listener as healthy.

title: Reject duplicate entity names in config mutations
description: PutFilter, PutIngester, PutVault, PutRoute, and PutRotationPolicy are upserts keyed by UUID but don't check for name uniqueness. This allows two entities of the same type to share a name, which is confusing in the UI and error-prone for scripted setup. Should return an error when a different entity of the same type already has the requested name.

title: Replace abandoned go-relp library with in-house RELP protocol implementation
description: The current RELP ingester depends on github.com/thierry-f-78/go-relp v1.0.0, which has only 1 commit and is completely abandoned. No other maintained Go RELP libraries exist. The RELP protocol is simple enough (text-based framing with 4 commands) to implement in-house in ~300-400 lines of pure Go, eliminating the external dependency entirely.

title: Support ordering search results by SourceTS and IngestTS
description:
Add query-level sort order support for SourceTS and IngestTS, not just the default WriteTS.

## Context

Records in chunks are physically ordered by WriteTS. Source and ingest timestamp indexes (`_source.idx`, `_ingest.idx`) already exist for sealed chunks with sorted `(TS, Pos)` entries and binary search via `FindSourceStartPosition` / `FindIngestStartPosition`. ChunkMeta tracks `SourceStart`/`SourceEnd` and `IngestStart`/`IngestEnd` bounds for chunk-level pruning.

## What's needed

1. **Index-walking cursor**: A new cursor type that iterates through the source/ingest index entries in TS order, seeking to each `Pos` in the chunk to read the full record. Replaces sequential Next/Prev for TS-ordered scans.
2. **Search engine plumbing**: Wire the index-walking cursor into the merge-sort heap so it compares by the selected timestamp field instead of WriteTS. New query directive: `order=source_ts` or `order=ingest_ts`.
3. **Active chunk handling**: Unsealed chunks have no TS indexes. Buffer matching records from active chunks and sort in memory before merging with sealed chunk results.
4. **Cloud vaults**: Fetch `_source.idx` / `_ingest.idx` alongside other index files (same pattern as existing indexes).
5. **Pipeline `RecordToRow`**: Expose `source_ts`, `ingest_ts`, and `write_ts` as row fields so `| sort source_ts` works in pipelines independently of this feature.
6. **Frontend**: Time range selector should indicate which timestamp field is being used for ordering.

title: Embed source and ingest TS indexes in GLCB cloud blob format
description:
Extend the GLCB blob format to include source and ingest timestamp indexes, enabling efficient time-range pruning for cloud vaults without full sequential scans.

## Context

Cloud vaults currently use `NoopIndexManager` — every query does a full scan. Token/attr/KV indexes would roughly double blob size and aren't worth it for archival storage. But TS indexes are tiny (12 bytes per record) and unlock time-range pruning within a chunk (binary search instead of full scan).

## Approach

Embed the indexes directly in the GLCB format with a section TOC. No sidecar objects, no local cache, no legacy migration (no legacy blobs exist).

Proposed layout:
```
Header | Dict | RecordIndex | Zstd Data | SourceTSIndex | IngestTSIndex | TOC | Footer
```

TOC enables range requests to read just the index sections without downloading the entire blob.

## Tasks

1. Extend GLCB format with TOC footer listing section offsets/sizes
2. Build source + ingest TS indexes during `ImportRecords` and append to blob
3. Implement `FindSourceStartPosition` / `FindIngestStartPosition` for cloud chunks using embedded indexes
4. Replace `NoopIndexManager` with a cloud index manager that reads from the blob's embedded indexes
5. Update `Reader` to parse TOC and provide index access
6. Update `Writer` to build and append TS indexes + TOC

title: Per-vault configurable index types
description:
Allow each vault to specify which index types are built for its sealed chunks. Enables tuning the storage/performance trade-off per use case (archival vs operational vs cloud).

## Context

Currently every sealed chunk gets the full index set (token, attr, KV, JSON path, source TS, ingest TS). The query engine already handles missing indexes gracefully — unsealed chunks never have them, so it falls back to runtime filtering. This means disabling an index just means slower queries, not broken ones.

## Use cases

- **Archival vaults**: TS indexes only — minimal storage overhead, time-range queries still fast
- **Operational vaults**: full index set for maximum query speed
- **Cloud vaults**: TS indexes only (token/attr/KV would double blob size)

## Tasks

1. Add `enabled_indexes` repeated string field to vault proto config
2. Default to all indexes enabled for backwards compatibility
3. Scheduler reads vault config and only builds enabled index types
4. Vault settings UI: checkbox list for index types with storage impact hints
5. Handle index deletion when a type is disabled (remove existing indexes for that type, or leave them and just stop building new ones)

title: Export search results into a vault
description:
Materialize search results (including cross-vault, cross-node, and pipeline-transformed) into a target vault. Enables pulling archived data from slow cloud storage into a local vault for fast indexed queries.

## Use cases

- Pull 2024 records from cloud archival vault into a local vault for heavy analysis
- Filter and transform records via pipeline, then persist the result set
- Create curated subsets of logs for incident investigation or compliance

## Design

- Pipeline sink syntax: `last=1y vault_id=archive ingester_type=kafka | export vault=analysis-2024`
- Preserves original timestamps (WriteTS, SourceTS, IngestTS) and attrs via AppendPreserved
- Runs as a background job with progress tracking via existing job system
- Target vault must already exist (no implicit creation)
- Deduplication via EventID to allow safe re-runs / resumption

## Tasks

1. New pipeline operator `export` with `vault` parameter
2. Backend job type that streams search results into target vault via AppendPreserved
3. Progress reporting: records exported, bytes, estimated completion
4. Cross-node support: export job runs on the node that owns the target vault, pulls from remote nodes as needed
5. UI: export button in search toolbar, vault selector, job progress in jobs panel

title: Ingest-time record enrichment via pipeline transforms on routes
description:
Allow routes to specify a pipeline transform that runs on every record before writing to the chunk store. Records arrive pre-enriched, fully indexed, and queryable without runtime lookups.

## Example

Route config:
- Match: ingester=nginx → vault=web-logs
- Transform: `| lookup geoip remote_host | rename remote_host=client_ip`

Every record stored in web-logs has geo fields (country, city, lat/lon) baked into attrs.

## Design considerations

- Transforms must be fast and non-blocking — the ingest path is latency-sensitive
- Failing transforms should not drop records (write original on error, flag with attr)
- Lookup tables need to be in-memory or very fast (no network round-trips per record)
- Config validation: reject transforms that are too expensive (aggregations, sorts, exports)
- Only a subset of pipeline operators make sense at ingest time: lookup, rename, extract, drop fields

## Depends on

- gastrolog-5laln (export search results into vault) establishes the pipeline-to-write-path plumbing

title: searchPipelineGlobal omits histogram for non-table results
description: server/query.go:199 — when searchPipelineGlobal returns non-table results (e.g. `| tail 100`, `| sort field`), the histogram is computed but not included in the final response. The single-node path (line 147) sends it correctly. Multi-node users running pipeline queries with sort/tail/slice won't see a histogram.

title: Sequential remote vault fetching in collectRemote
description: server/query.go:524 — collectRemote fetches vaults one-by-one in a nested loop (nodes × vaults). With N remote vaults, that's N serial RPCs. Should fan out concurrently with errgroup. Same pattern in collectRemotePipeline (line 672).

title: Histogram bucket sort overflows on 32-bit int
description: server/query.go:583 — mergeHistogramBuckets sort uses int(x.TimestampMs - y.TimestampMs) which overflows when int is 32 bits. Fix: use cmp.Compare(x.TimestampMs, y.TimestampMs).

title: forwardRemote under exclusive lock has no timeout
description: orchestrator/ingest.go:115 — forwardRemote uses context.Background() while holding o.mu.Lock(). The forwarder contract says "must be non-blocking" but nothing enforces it. A blocking forwarder would deadlock the entire orchestrator (no searches, no config ops). Add a timeout wrapper or channel-based enforcement.

title: avg() merge fallback silently sums instead of erroring
description: server/query_merge.go:76 — the fallback for avg() in the merge strategy treats it as sum, which is mathematically wrong. The code assumes PipelineNeedsGlobalRecords routes all avg() queries through the global path, but if that routing has a gap, results will be silently incorrect. Should panic or return an error instead of falling back to sum.

title: NewConfigServer has 11 positional parameters
description: server/config.go:58 — the constructor takes 11 positional parameters, making call sites fragile (easy to swap two parameters of the same type). Refactor to a Config struct, consistent with how orchestrator.Config is used.

title: tail/slice pipelines materialize all records into heap
description:
\`applyRecordOps\` (pipeline_ops.go:19-26) drains the entire search iterator into a \`[]chunk.Record\` slice with \`.Copy()\` on every record before any operator runs. For \`| tail 10\` on 1M records, this copies all 1M into heap, materializes all fields, then takes the last 10.

**Fix:** detect tail-only and slice-only pipelines (optional where/eval before tail/slice, no sort/stats) and use bounded iteration:

- **tail N**: ring buffer of size N during iteration. Apply pre-tail where/eval inline, keep only the last N survivors.
- **slice M-N**: skip iterator up to position M-1, collect N-M+1 records, stop. Pre-slice where/eval applied inline.

Both avoid full materialization. The iterator (\`iter.Seq2[chunk.Record, error]\`) already supports this — the optimization is in \`applyRecordOps\` choosing a streaming path when the pipeline shape allows it.

Sort still requires full materialization (unavoidable without an index-backed order).

title: Kafka ingester tight-loops on fetch errors with no backoff
description:
When a Kafka topic doesn't exist (or any persistent fetch error), the consume loop in \`ingester/kafka/ingester.go\` spins at full speed — thousands of warnings per second. \`PollFetches\` returns immediately on error, and the loop has no sleep/backoff before retrying.

**Fix:** Add exponential backoff (e.g. 100ms → 1s → 5s cap) when a fetch cycle returns only errors and zero records. Reset the backoff on a successful fetch. This prevents log flooding and CPU waste while still recovering quickly when the topic becomes available.

title: Handle archived cloud blobs gracefully (S3 Glacier + Azure Archive)
description:
When S3 lifecycle policies transition GLCB blobs to Glacier Flexible Retrieval or Deep Archive, \`GetObject\` returns \`InvalidObjectState\`. Currently this surfaces as a raw AWS SDK error and breaks the entire search.

**Phase 1 (this issue):**
- Detect \`InvalidObjectState\` in \`S3Store.Download()\` and return a typed \`ErrBlobArchived\` error.
- In the cloud vault search path, skip archived chunks gracefully instead of failing the query.
- Log a warning with the chunk ID and storage class.
- Include a count of skipped archived chunks in the search response so the UI can show "N chunks excluded (archived)".

**Phase 2 (separate issue, later):**
- Check storage class on \`Head()\`/\`ListObjects\` and tag chunks as archived.
- Show archive status in the vault detail UI.
- Initiate \`RestoreObject\` from the UI with progress tracking.

Note: Glacier Instant Retrieval works transparently with normal S3 GET — no special handling needed. Only Flexible Retrieval and Deep Archive require restore-before-read.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: Testing strategy: MinIO doesn't support Glacier storage classes. Use mock-based tests — inject a fake S3 client that returns InvalidObjectState on GetObject and the right StorageClass on HeadObject/ListObjectsV2. LocalStack Pro supports Glacier but isn't worth the dependency. Detection approach: ListObjectsV2 already returns StorageClass per object, so we can flag archived keys during existing vault scans without extra API calls — cheaper than catching errors on download.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: Azure Blob Storage has the same problem. Archive tier objects are offline — GetBlob fails until rehydrated (SetBlobTier back to Hot/Cool, takes 1-15h). Lifecycle management can silently transition blobs just like S3. ListBlobs returns AccessTier per blob, so same detection approach works. GCS is safe — all storage classes are immediately readable, just higher retrieval costs. The fix needs to cover both S3 (StorageClass) and Azure (AccessTier). azure.go currently has no archive tier handling.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Design: Generic chunk availability at the manager level.

ChunkAvailability enum: ChunkHot (immediately readable), ChunkCold (archived, needs restore). No need to model fine-grained tiers — gastrolog only cares whether it can read the chunk right now.

Memory/file managers: always ChunkHot. GCS cloud manager: always ChunkHot. S3 cloud manager: maps StorageClass (GLACIER, DEEP_ARCHIVE → ChunkCold, everything else → ChunkHot). Azure cloud manager: maps AccessTier (Archive → ChunkCold, everything else → ChunkHot).

Mapping logic stays in the cloud manager — it knows which tier values are readable. Query layer just sees hot/cold, skips cold chunks, and reports a count: 'searched 340 chunks, 47 archived (not searchable)'.

title: Vault rechunking — repack sealed chunks into different sizes
description:
Sealed chunks are immutable after creation, so if the operator changes chunk dimensioning, historical data retains the old sizing forever. This is especially costly for cloud vaults where chunk size directly impacts GET request counts and transfer costs.

Add a vault maintenance job that repacks sealed chunks into new target dimensions. Records are already in write-timestamp order, so repacking is concatenation — no sorting. Write new chunks alongside old ones, swap the manifest, then delete originals.

title: Timestamp restructuring: IngestTS as primary query timestamp
description:
Restructure how records and chunks are timestamped, ordered, indexed, and queried. Switch the primary query timestamp from WriteTS to IngestTS. WriteTS becomes chunk-local (not preserved across migrations). This unblocks route-based eject (gastrolog-1f3jp) and vault rechunking (gastrolog-3c0a6).

## Motivation

WriteTS is stamped at append time and is monotonic within a chunk, enabling fast binary search on active chunks. But it creates fundamental problems when moving records between chunks:

- Ejecting records from one vault to another via routes resets WriteTS, breaking search accuracy
- Rechunking requires decomposing and re-sealing, which also resets WriteTS
- Preserving WriteTS across moves breaks monotonicity when records from different sources interleave

IngestTS is immutable — it's a property of the record, not the storage location. Switching to it as the primary query timestamp decouples search from storage topology.

## Key challenge

IngestTS is not monotonic on active chunks. Two ingesters writing to the same vault (one local, one remote) produce constantly out-of-order IngestTS. Binary search on idx.log no longer works. Solution: mmap'd B+ tree file indexes on active chunks for IngestTS and SourceTS.

## Scope

- B+ tree file index implementation (new data structure)
- Active chunk B+ tree indexing (integrate into write path)
- Query engine switch (IngestTS as primary chunk pre-filter)
- Rename StartTS/EndTS to WriteStart/WriteEnd (disambiguation)
- Demote WriteTS to chunk-local (not preserved across moves, retention clock resets)
- Route-based eject (gastrolog-1f3jp) — unblocked by WriteTS demotion
- Vault rechunking (gastrolog-3c0a6) — unblocked by WriteTS demotion

## What's NOT affected

- Attribute dictionary and attr.log — append-order, no timestamp dependency
- Sealed chunk IngestTS/SourceTS indexes — already exist as sorted arrays
- Cloud vaults — sealed-only, no active chunk; minor changes to ImportRecords re-stamping and chunk selection

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
## I/O strategy for B+ tree indexes

Active chunks use B+ tree file indexes (pread/pwrite with in-process page cache) for IngestTS and SourceTS. No mmap — active chunks grow continuously and macOS lacks mremap.

Sealed chunks already have dense sorted timestamp indexes (_ingest.idx, _source.idx) — flat sorted arrays of (ts, pos) pairs built at seal time. No changes needed there.

## Duplicate timestamps

IngestTS is not unique — two records from the same or different ingesters can share a nanosecond timestamp. This is fine:
- B+ tree uses non-unique keys (duplicates allowed in leaves)
- Range queries walk the linked leaf chain, visiting all duplicates
- EventID (IngesterID, IngestTS, IngestSeq) provides true uniqueness when needed
- Sealed chunk dense sorted arrays already handle duplicates via sort stability (secondary sort on record position)

title: B+ tree file index implementation
description:
Implement an mmap'd B+ tree file supporting random-order inserts of (timestamp, position) pairs and fast range queries. This is the foundation for active chunk indexing on non-monotonic timestamps (IngestTS, SourceTS).

## Requirements

- Insert: O(log n), any order — no monotonicity assumption
- Range query: O(log n) seek + linear leaf walk via linked leaf chain
- Point lookup: O(log n)
- Storage: single mmap'd file per index, grows by appending pages on splits
- Generic over timestamp type to serve both IngestTS and SourceTS

## Structure

- Internal nodes: keys (timestamps) + child page pointers for navigation
- Leaf nodes: sorted (timestamp, record_position) entries + next-leaf pointer
- Leaf chain forms a sorted linked list for linear range scans after initial seek

## No background maintenance

Unlike LSM, B+ tree has no compaction or merge threads. The OS page cache handles hot node caching via mmap.

Part of epic gastrolog-5mzlf.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
## I/O strategy: pread/pwrite for active, mmap for sealed

Active chunk B+ trees use pread/pwrite with a small in-process page cache (internal nodes are hot, leaf nodes fall through to OS file cache). No mmap growth/remap issues, no macOS portability problems (no mremap).

Sealed chunk B+ trees (built at seal time or inherited from the active phase) are read-only and fixed-size — ideal for mmap. OS manages the page cache, zero-copy reads.

This split matches the existing pattern: active chunks use file I/O for writes, sealed chunks use mmap for reads.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Correction to c1: B+ tree indexes only exist on active chunks. Sealed chunks already have sparse time indexes (_time.idx) built at seal time — those handle non-monotonic IngestTS fine since they're constructed in one pass over the final data.

So the I/O strategy is simply pread/pwrite with in-process page cache. No mmap involved.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Correction to c1/c2: sealed chunks already have dense sorted timestamp indexes (_ingest.idx, _source.idx) — flat sorted arrays of (ts, pos) pairs with binary search. Every record is indexed, not sparse. These are built at seal time by iterating the cursor, sorting, and writing.

The B+ tree is only needed for active chunks, where records arrive continuously and can't be batch-sorted. Active chunks need an index structure that supports incremental inserts in non-monotonic order with efficient range queries. That's the B+ tree.

I/O: pread/pwrite with in-process page cache. No mmap.

title: Active chunk B+ tree indexing
description:
Integrate B+ tree file indexes into the active chunk write path. Each active chunk maintains two B+ trees — one for IngestTS and one for SourceTS — updated on every Append call.

## Write path changes

- On Append: insert (IngestTS, position) and (SourceTS, position) into respective B+ trees
- B+ tree files live alongside idx.log, raw.log, attr.log, attr_dict.log in the chunk directory
- Insert happens in Phase 2 (outside mutex), serialized by writeMu like the other file writes

## Seal time

- Flatten B+ trees into existing sorted-array index format (tsidx ingest/source indexes)
- Or keep B+ tree files as-is if the sorted-array format is no longer needed
- Delete B+ tree files after sealed indexes are built

## Active chunk queries

- Time-range queries on active chunks use B+ tree seek instead of WriteTS binary search on idx.log
- Returns record positions for cursor iteration

Part of epic gastrolog-5mzlf. Depends on gastrolog-1enrp (B+ tree implementation).

title: Query engine: switch primary timestamp to IngestTS
description:
Change the primary query timestamp from WriteTS to IngestTS throughout the query engine.

## Chunk pre-filter

- Primary chunk selection switches from StartTS/EndTS (WriteTS bounds) to IngestStart/IngestEnd
- WriteTS bounds become a secondary/optional filter
- Query.Start/End fields now represent IngestTS bounds, not WriteTS

## Active chunk queries

- Use B+ tree IngestTS index for range seeks instead of WriteTS binary search on idx.log
- SourceTS queries use B+ tree SourceTS index

## Sealed chunk queries

- Continue using existing sorted-array IngestTS index (already built at seal time)
- No change to sealed chunk read path

## Cloud chunk queries

- Chunk selection uses IngestStart/IngestEnd from blob header / object metadata
- Already stored — no format change needed

Part of epic gastrolog-5mzlf. Depends on gastrolog-3trcd (active chunk B+ tree indexing).

title: Rename StartTS/EndTS to WriteStart/WriteEnd
description:
Rename StartTS/EndTS to WriteStart/WriteEnd throughout the stack for disambiguation. Currently ambiguous — could be mistaken for IngestTS or SourceTS bounds. Consistent with existing IngestStart/IngestEnd and SourceStart/SourceEnd naming.

## Scope

- ChunkMeta struct fields
- Proto definitions + regenerate
- GLCB header format
- S3/Azure/GCS object metadata keys (start_ts/end_ts → write_start/write_end)
- Query planner and chunk selection code
- Retention policy evaluation
- Rotation policy evaluation
- Any UI or API surface that exposes these fields

Rename through the entire stack — no partial renames.

Part of epic gastrolog-5mzlf. Depends on gastrolog-5sabn (query engine switch — do the rename after to avoid merge conflicts).

title: Demote WriteTS to chunk-local
description:
WriteTS becomes a chunk-local timestamp — stamped at append time, not preserved across migration, eject, or rechunk. It remains useful for:

- Append-order key in idx.log (cheap sequential access within a chunk)
- Rotation policy input (chunk age decisions)
- Retention TTL on the current vault (clock resets when records move to a new vault)

## Changes

- MoveChunk: record-level import path re-stamps WriteTS at destination write time
- ImportRecords: re-stamps WriteTS (already the natural behavior for new appends)
- RemoteTransferrer: re-stamps WriteTS on receiving node
- GLCB writer (cloud vaults): re-stamps WriteTS when building blob from ejected/migrated records
- Retention TTL: counts from destination chunk's WriteTS, not original. Operator thinks "keep for 3 months in this vault" not "3 months since original ingestion"

## What stays the same

- WriteTS is still written to idx.log and GLCB record frames
- WriteTS bounds still tracked in ChunkMeta (as WriteStart/WriteEnd after rename)
- Filesystem rename fast path (MoveChunk) still preserves WriteTS (chunk moves whole, no re-stamping)

Part of epic gastrolog-5mzlf. Depends on gastrolog-5sabn (query engine must not rely on WriteTS as primary before we stop preserving it).

title: Frontend quality audit: accessibility, theming, performance, responsive
description: Comprehensive frontend quality audit covering 33 issues across 5 dimensions: accessibility (contrast, ARIA, keyboard nav), theming (hard-coded colors, missing tokens, palette support), performance (layout thrashing, passive listeners), responsive design (mobile sizing), and anti-patterns. The design system is fundamentally strong — most issues are about extending existing patterns to places that were missed.
acceptance: All critical and high-severity issues resolved. Ghost/muted text contrast meets WCAG AA. No hard-coded Tailwind standard colors. All interactive elements have proper ARIA labels. Theme tokens cover all semantic color needs. Charts adapt to palette changes.

title: Ghost text contrast ratio ~1.2:1 fails WCAG A
description: CSS variable --color-text-ghost (#656058 on #0d0f12 dark, #958e84 on #f4f0ea light) has contrast ratio ~1.2:1 (dark) and ~1.5:1 (light). WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 minimum for normal text. This affects StatPill labels, TimeRangePicker, Sidebar, placeholders sitewide. Defined in frontend/src/styles/main.css lines 23 and 52. Must be adjusted across all 10 palette definitions.
acceptance: Ghost text meets WCAG AA 4.5:1 contrast ratio in all palettes (both dark and light modes). Verify with a contrast checker tool.

title: Muted text contrast ratio ~2.8:1 fails WCAG AA
description: CSS variable --color-text-muted (#8a8480 on dark backgrounds) has contrast ratio ~2.8:1. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 minimum. Used extensively for form labels, field descriptions, and badges throughout the UI. Defined in frontend/src/styles/main.css. Recommendation: lighten to ~#a8a29e for 4.5:1 on #0d0f12. Must be adjusted across all 10 palette definitions.
acceptance: Muted text meets WCAG AA 4.5:1 contrast ratio in all palettes (both dark and light modes).

title: Hard-coded Tailwind red/green colors bypass theme system
description: 11 instances of text-red-400/text-red-600 and text-green-400/text-green-600 bypass the design token system. These don't change with palette — Nord, Dracula, etc. get jarring standard Tailwind red/green instead of muted severity tokens. Files: IngestersSettings.tsx:207,326, RouteStatsView.tsx:54,210-211, PoliciesSettings.tsx:120,124, Buttons.tsx:36 (danger variant), VaultParamsForm.tsx:241-242, TestConnectionButton.tsx:59-60. Replace with semantic severity tokens (--color-severity-error, --color-severity-info).
acceptance: Zero instances of text-red-* or text-green-* Tailwind standard colors in frontend/src/components/. All replaced with semantic severity tokens from the design system.

title: Mermaid diagram colors are Observatory-only
description: Mermaid.tsx:15-57 has 36 hard-coded hex values for dark/light variants, all Observatory palette colors. The other 9 palettes (Nord, Solarized, Dracula, Catppuccin, Gruvbox, Tokyo Night, Rosé Pine, Everforest, Synthwave) all show Observatory-colored diagrams. Should read CSS variables at render time to adapt to the active palette.
acceptance: Mermaid diagrams visually adapt to the active palette. No hard-coded Observatory hex values in Mermaid.tsx.

title: Missing text-on-copper semantic token
description: 8 components independently solve 'what color text goes on a copper button?' with inconsistent answers (text-ink, text-white, or nothing). Files: ChangePasswordDialog.tsx:143, TimeRangePicker.tsx:137,253,280, SearchResults.tsx:158-159, SetupWizard.tsx:264, AuthPage.tsx:236, Buttons.tsx:33, Checkbox.tsx:27, SavedQueries.tsx:78. Need a --color-text-on-copper token in main.css and standardize all copper button text to use it.
acceptance: A --color-text-on-copper token exists in main.css for all palettes. All copper-background buttons use this token. Consistent appearance across dark/light modes.

title: Missing light-mode variants on copper buttons
description: 5 components use single-variant classes like 'bg-copper text-white' without the c() theme helper, so they don't adapt to light mode. Files: QueryBar.tsx:497,507, SetupWizard.tsx:264, Buttons.tsx:33,36, SavedQueries.tsx:78. Should use c('dark-variant', 'light-variant') pattern for all themed elements.
acceptance: All copper/danger buttons use the c() theme helper with both dark and light variants. No single-variant color classes on interactive elements.
notes: Depends on text-on-copper token being created first.

title: ECharts hard-coded RGBA fallbacks don't adapt to palettes
description: 20+ instances of hard-coded rgba() values in chart components used as fallbacks for CSS variables. Tooltip borders, text colors, and the heatmap gradient don't adapt when users switch palettes. Files: HistogramChart.tsx:322-323,360, DonutChart.tsx:38-39, HeatmapChart.tsx:52,54,113,117 (heatmap gradient is entirely static: #313695,#4575b4,#74add1,#fee090,#f46d43,#d73027), WorldMapChart.tsx:357-362, TimeSeriesChart.tsx:124,143, echartsTheme.ts:14-15,21-22,70.
acceptance: Chart components read all colors from CSS variables with no hard-coded RGBA fallbacks. Heatmap gradient adapts to the active palette.

title: Missing aria-labels on interactive controls
description: 6 components have interactive controls without proper aria-labels: (1) TimeRangePicker.tsx:206-220 — prev/next month buttons use Unicode arrows only, need aria-label='Previous/Next month'. (2) TableView.tsx:57-73 — sortable column headers have onClick but no aria-label or sort direction announcement. (3) SavedQueries.tsx:58-73 — name input has placeholder but no aria-label. (4) TimeRangePicker.tsx:162-174,187-199 — time inputs have placeholder 'HH:mm' but no aria-label='Start/End time'. (5) FormField.tsx:309-330 — ParamsEditor key/value inputs lack labels. (6) FileDropZone.tsx:87-109 — drop area has no aria-label.
acceptance: All 6 components have proper aria-labels on every interactive control. Screen readers can identify every button, input, and interactive region.

title: Sidebar width transitions cause layout thrashing
description: Two sidebar components animate width changes using transition-[width], which triggers full reflow on every animation frame. Files: SearchSidebar.tsx:94 and DetailSidebar.tsx:28 both use 'transition-[width] duration-200'. Should use transform-based approach (e.g. transform: translateX() with overflow hidden) or position: fixed containers to avoid layout recalculation.
acceptance: Sidebar collapse/expand animations no longer trigger layout reflows. Use Chrome DevTools Performance panel to verify — no red layout shift markers during sidebar animation.

title: Non-passive event listeners may block compositor
description: Event listeners added without { passive: true } flag can block the compositor thread during interaction. Files: HistogramChart.tsx:420-421,475-476 (mousemove/mouseup during brush/pan) and QueryBar.tsx:181-183 (mousedown/mouseup/click for outside-click detection). The QueryBar listeners don't call preventDefault, so they should be passive.
acceptance: All event listeners that don't call preventDefault use { passive: true }. HistogramChart brush handlers evaluated case-by-case.

title: pointer-events-none disabled pattern breaks keyboard a11y
description: 3 components use 'opacity-50 pointer-events-none' to disable sections without aria-disabled='true'. Keyboard users can still focus elements inside but they don't respond — creating zombie interactive elements. Files: SettingsSection.tsx:67, ServiceSettings.tsx:236,325. Should use aria-disabled='true' on the container and disabled attribute on individual form controls.
acceptance: Disabled sections use aria-disabled='true'. Keyboard users cannot focus controls inside disabled sections. Screen readers announce disabled state.

title: ExpandableCard header missing role='button'
description: ExpandableCard.tsx:42-50 — the clickable header div has aria-expanded but no role='button' or tabIndex={0}. Screen readers don't announce it as interactive. Should add role='button' and tabIndex={0}, or replace the div with a <button> element.
acceptance: ExpandableCard header is announced as a button by screen readers, is keyboard-focusable, and toggleable with Enter/Space.

title: Skip link and main.css use hard-coded colors
description: main.css:620 uses 'color: #fff' on the skip link focus state and main.css:625 uses 'box-shadow: rgba(0,0,0,0.3)'. These don't adapt to palette. Minor impact since skip link is rarely visible, but breaks the 'no raw hex values' principle.
acceptance: Skip link uses design tokens for color and shadow. No hard-coded #fff or rgba() in main.css outside of token definitions.

title: useMemo usage violates React Compiler rule
description: React Compiler is enabled in this project — useMemo is redundant and can conflict with compiler optimizations. 2 components still use it: HelpDialog.tsx:82 (searchResults memo) and TableView.tsx:27-35,37-50 (isNumeric and sortedRows memos). Remove the useMemo wrappers and let the compiler handle memoization.
acceptance: Zero useMemo calls in the codebase. grep -r 'useMemo' frontend/src/ returns no results.

title: Dialog padding doesn't scale for mobile
description: Dialog.tsx:6-8 uses p-6 (24px) padding at all viewport sizes. On phones, both sides consume 48px leaving ~50vw usable content. The lg size (max-w-4xl) and xl size (max-w-5xl) are fine for desktop but settings/inspector dialogs become cramped on smaller screens. Should use responsive padding: p-3 sm:p-6 or similar.
acceptance: Dialog padding scales down on mobile viewports. Content area is usable on 375px-wide screens.

title: Toast max-width exceeds small phone viewports
description: Toast.tsx:126 uses 'fixed bottom-4 right-4' with 'max-w-sm' (384px). On phones under 400px wide, toasts are clipped or cause horizontal overflow. Should use max-w-[calc(100vw-2rem)] sm:max-w-sm to cap width on small viewports.
acceptance: Toasts are fully visible on 320px-wide viewports without horizontal overflow.

title: UserMenu dropdown min-width overflows narrow viewports
description: UserMenu.tsx:46 uses min-w-48 (192px) on the dropdown. Also HeaderBar.tsx:243 uses min-w-40 (160px) on node stats tooltip, and Buttons.tsx:92 uses min-w-[10rem] (160px) on dropdown menu. These fixed minimums cause horizontal overflow on narrow viewports.
acceptance: Dropdown menus don't cause horizontal overflow on 320px viewports. Use max-w constraints or responsive min-widths.

title: Modal overlay uses hard-coded black regardless of palette
description: Dialog.tsx:54 uses bg-black/40 and SearchView.tsx:42,300 uses bg-black/30 for modal overlays. These don't adapt to palette. Should use a design token like --color-overlay that defaults to black but could be themed.
acceptance: Modal overlay color uses a design token. Appearance is consistent across palettes.

title: Ternary operator instead of useThemeClass() helper
description: IngestersSettings.tsx:207,326 uses inline ternary operators for dark/light class selection instead of the useThemeClass(dark) helper pattern used everywhere else. Inconsistent code style that makes theming harder to audit.
acceptance: IngestersSettings.tsx uses the c() helper from useThemeClass for all dark/light class selection. No inline ternary operators for theme classes.

title: Fixed-width numeric inputs don't scale for mobile
description: FormField.tsx:284,314 uses 'min-w-20 w-20' (80px) on numeric inputs. On a 375px phone this is 21% of viewport. Should use responsive sizing: min-w-16 md:w-20 or let them flex.
acceptance: Numeric inputs are usable on 375px viewports without disproportionate sizing.

title: CSV table max-width truncates content unnecessarily
description: CsvSection.tsx:167 uses max-w-[200px] on CSV table cells combined with truncate. On mobile this is wider than content areas, and on desktop it hides data that could wrap instead of truncating.
acceptance: CSV table cells use appropriate text handling — truncate with tooltip or wrap based on available space.

title: ExplainPanel predicate display too narrow on tablets
description: ExplainPanel.tsx:702 uses max-w-[60%] on predicate display in query plan. On narrow tablets (<600px) this leaves only ~180px for content. On phone viewports (<375px) the predicate becomes unreadable.
acceptance: Predicate display is readable on 375px viewports. Uses responsive max-width or removes constraint on small screens.

title: Settings/Inspector sidebar needs responsive collapse
description: SettingsDialog.tsx:125 and InspectorDialog.tsx:148 use min-w-fit on tab sidebars. On tablet viewports (max-width: 1023px), these sidebars should collapse or switch to a horizontal tab bar. Currently they consume fixed width regardless of viewport size.
acceptance: Settings and Inspector dialog sidebars adapt to tablet viewports — either collapse to icons, switch to horizontal tabs, or use a drawer pattern.

title: backdrop-blur on opaque badge is unnecessary
description: SearchResults.tsx:157 — the 'N new logs' floating badge uses backdrop-blur with bg-copper/90 (nearly opaque). The blur has no visible effect since the background is 90% opaque, but it costs GPU resources and reads as a 'trendy template' pattern inconsistent with the Observatory design language.
acceptance: backdrop-blur removed from the new-logs badge. If translucency is desired, reduce bg opacity to make blur visible; otherwise remove blur entirely.

title: Semantic span role='button' should be actual button elements
description: FormField.tsx:68-78 uses <span role='button'> for ExampleValues clickable elements instead of semantic <button> elements. Native buttons provide keyboard handling, focus management, and screen reader semantics for free. Also QueryHistory.tsx:50-55 uses div with onClick instead of button.
acceptance: ExampleValues in FormField and QueryHistory items use <button> elements. No span or div with role='button' remains.

title: Missing will-change on sidebar animations
description: SearchSidebar and DetailSidebar animate width changes but don't hint the browser with will-change: width. Adding will-change promotes the element to its own compositor layer, reducing paint cost during animation. Remove will-change after animation completes to free GPU memory.
acceptance: Sidebar elements have will-change applied during animation and removed after.

title: Box-shadow on toasts multiplies with count
description: Toast.tsx:160 applies shadow-lg to each toast. When multiple toasts stack, each adds its own shadow layer, creating overlapping GPU paint areas. Consider using a single shadow on the toast container instead, or only applying shadow to the topmost toast.
acceptance: Toast rendering doesn't create excessive overlapping shadows when multiple toasts are visible.

title: rounded-full on small icon buttons feels inconsistent
description: Dialog.tsx:71 (close button), HelpButton.tsx:11, SearchResults.tsx:157 ('N new logs' badge), and various icon buttons use rounded-full (100% border radius). This creates a toy-like softness inconsistent with the Observatory design's more angular aesthetic. Consider rounded-md or rounded for small buttons. Low priority — purely aesthetic.
acceptance: Small icon buttons use rounded-md or rounded consistently, unless they are intentionally circular (avatars, dot indicators).

title: Hero-sized 3.5em on single-value display
description: PipelineResults.tsx:73-80 renders single-value pipeline results at text-[3.5em] font-mono font-semibold. This is hero-metric territory — visually impactful but reads as 'dashboard template energy'. The label below provides context, so the oversized number is purely decorative. Consider reducing to ~2.5em.
acceptance: Single-value display uses a more restrained font size (2-2.5em range) that's still prominent without being gratuitous.

title: 'Waiting for data...' lacks context
description: SystemStatsView.tsx:26 shows 'Waiting for data...' without indicating what data. In a stats view, this should say 'Waiting for node stats...' or similar to help users understand what's loading.
acceptance: Loading message specifies what data is being awaited.

title: DetailPanel w-[1%] key column trick is fragile
description: DetailPanel.tsx:36 uses w-[1%] CSS trick to minimize key column width combined with whitespace-nowrap. On extreme narrow viewports (<280px), long keys will overflow. Low risk since the detail panel is only visible on wider viewports, but the pattern is fragile.
acceptance: Key column uses a more robust sizing approach (e.g. w-auto with max-w constraint).

title: Auth page missing explicit mobile min-height
description: AuthPage.tsx:147 has good responsive width (w-full max-w-sm) and padding (px-4), but no explicit min-height. On short landscape phones the login form may not fill the viewport properly.
acceptance: Auth page renders correctly on landscape phones (e.g. 667x375 viewport).

title: HelpDialog SVG toggle lacks proper focus styling
description: HelpDialog.tsx:262-274 has an SVG with role='button' and onKeyDown handler but lacks proper focus-visible styling. Keyboard users can activate it but don't see a focus indicator. Should add focus-visible:ring or outline styling.
acceptance: Help dialog toggle SVG shows a visible focus indicator when focused via keyboard.

title: Design system normalization: severity contrast, validation colors, spacing
description:
Normalize frontend components to design system standards. Key findings:

1. **text-white on severity backgrounds fails WCAG** across 8/10 palettes — needs per-severity text color tokens
2. **text-amber-500** used for validation errors instead of design system severity-warn token
3. **Arbitrary padding values** (py-[8.5px], py-[9px]) instead of standard Tailwind scale

Found via systematic normalization analysis of all frontend components.

title: text-white on severity toggle buttons fails WCAG on 8/10 palettes
description:
severity.ts toggleActive classes use text-white for all 5 severity levels. WCAG contrast analysis shows white text fails on most palettes:

- error: white fails on 7/10 palettes (Catppuccin #f38ba8 → 2.3:1)
- warn: white fails on ALL 10 palettes (worst: Catppuccin #f9e2af → 1.3:1)
- info: white fails on 9/10 palettes
- debug: white fails on 8/10 palettes
- trace: mixed (5 pass, 5 fail)

Fix: Add --color-severity-X-text tokens to each palette in main.css, choosing white or ink based on which gives ≥4.5:1 contrast. Then use these in severity.ts toggleActive classes and danger button variants.

Files: frontend/src/lib/severity.ts, frontend/src/styles/main.css, frontend/src/components/settings/Buttons.tsx, frontend/src/components/QueryBar.tsx

title: Validation errors use text-amber-500 instead of design system severity-warn
description:
ServiceSettings.tsx uses Tailwind's text-amber-500 for 5 validation error messages. This is not a design system token and doesn't adapt to palette changes.

Should use text-severity-warn which is defined per-palette in main.css.

File: frontend/src/components/settings/ServiceSettings.tsx (lines 253, 275, 277, 550, 552)

title: Arbitrary padding values in QueryInput and QueryBar
description:
QueryInput.tsx uses py-[8.5px] (lines 219, 228) and QueryBar.tsx uses py-[9px] (line 222) instead of Tailwind's standard spacing scale.

Should use py-2 (8px) or py-2.5 (10px) from the standard scale. The query input and its collapsed placeholder need matching heights, so both should use the same standard value.

Files: frontend/src/components/QueryInput.tsx, frontend/src/components/QueryBar.tsx

title: Display timestamp matching order= directive in search results
description: The left-side timestamp column in search results always shows IngestTS. When order=source_ts or order=write_ts is used, it should display the corresponding timestamp instead.

title: Unify vault types: file vaults with selectable sealed chunk backing (local/S3/GCS/Azure)
description:
Replace the separate cloud vault type with a configurable sealed storage backend on file vaults. Currently cloud vaults are a distinct type that is sealed-only (no Append, no active chunk), which creates special-casing throughout the system — eject can't deliver to them via the normal Append path, they can't receive live ingestion, and they need separate code paths everywhere.

**Proposed model:** File vaults always have a local active chunk. When a chunk seals and compresses, the blob is pushed to the configured backing store: local disk (default), S3, GCS, or Azure. The GLCB block-level fetch already handles cloud reads efficiently, so the read path is solved.

**What changes:**
- File vault gains a "sealed storage" config: local (default), s3, gcs, azure — with the same provider params currently on cloud vaults (bucket, region, credentials, etc.)
- After compression, a new upload step pushes the blob to the configured backend and removes the local copy
- Cloud vault type is removed — existing cloud vault configs migrate to file vaults with cloud sealed storage
- All special-casing for sealed-only vaults disappears (ErrSealedOnly, sealed-only checks in eject, help docs, UI)
- Cloud-backed vaults can receive live ingestion directly, not just eject

**What stays the same:**
- Memory vaults (ephemeral, in-memory — different purpose)
- GLCB format, block compression, cloud readers
- All existing cloud provider implementations (S3, GCS, Azure)
- Rotation policies, retention policies, eject — all work uniformly

**Motivation:** The eject feature (gastrolog-1f3jp) delivers records via Append(), which cloud vaults don't support. Rather than adding special cases to detect cloud destinations and fall back to ImportRecords, unifying the vault model eliminates the problem entirely.

title: Vault replication across cluster nodes
description:
Research how to replicate vault data across multiple nodes for fault tolerance. Splunk-style model: primary node handles writes and queries, replicas receive forwarded records asynchronously and maintain independent chunk lifecycles. On primary failure, a replica is promoted.

Key design questions:
- Vault config: replica set (primary + replicas) vs replication factor
- Replication transport: forward records async from primary to replicas after Append
- Independent chunking: each node seals/compresses independently, same records different chunk boundaries
- Failover: Raft-based promotion, accept small data loss window from async replication
- Query path: primary-only (no dedup needed), replicas only on failover
- EventID as natural dedup key if reconciliation is ever needed
- Interaction with sealed backing (cloud storage) — replicas may not need their own cloud copies

title: Convert ForwardSearch from unary to server-streaming RPC
description:
ForwardSearch is currently a unary gRPC RPC — the remote node buffers all matching records into a single response before sending. This means the entire result set lives in memory on the remote node before a single byte is transmitted.

gRPC supports server-streaming over multiplexed HTTP/2. The public Search RPC already streams to the frontend via Connect. ForwardSearch should do the same: stream records as they're found, eliminating the memory spike from buffering.

## What to change

1. **Proto**: Change `ForwardSearch` from unary to server-streaming in `cluster.proto`
2. **SearchExecutor**: Change from returning a slice to accepting a callback or returning an iterator
3. **Cluster handler**: `forwardSearch` streams records via `grpc.ServerStream` instead of collecting into a slice
4. **SearchForwarder**: Client-side consumes the stream, feeding records to `collectRemote`
5. **collectRemote**: Adapts to streaming — no more resume tokens / pagination loop for ForwardSearch
6. **Remove drainRemoteRecords**: The pagination loop in export.go becomes unnecessary
7. **Tests**: Update multinode test harness (`directRemoteSearcher`)

## Why

- Removes memory proportional to result set on remote nodes (currently buffers everything)
- Eliminates the need for batch caps, resume tokens, and pagination loops in internal RPCs
- Aligns internal forwarding with the public Search RPC's streaming model
- The removed `maxBatch = 500` cap was masking this design gap — without it, large exports or queries buffer unbounded records on the remote node before responding

## Context

Discovered during gastrolog-5laln (export to vault). The hardcoded `maxBatch = 500` in `newSearchExecutor` was capping ForwardSearch responses, causing exports to silently truncate at 500 records per vault. Removing the cap fixes exports but exposes the underlying buffering issue.

title: Restrict JWT secret visibility, add regenerate button
description: The JWT secret is currently too exposed — it's exported and accessible in the frontend. It should be internal-only (not returned in any config/settings response). Add a dedicated RPC + frontend button to regenerate the JWT secret, which force-invalidates all active tokens cluster-wide.

title: Cache cloud chunk metadata in local B+ tree index
description: Cloud-backed vaults call store.List() + N×store.Head() at startup to rebuild the in-memory chunk metadata map. This is wasteful — chunk metadata is immutable once sealed and uploaded. Instead, maintain a local B+ tree index (using the existing btree package) that maps ChunkID → ChunkMeta. On upload, insert the metadata into the tree. On startup, scan the tree instead of querying the cloud. Only fall back to cloud listing when the index file is missing (first migration).

title: LRU cache for cloud chunk metadata instead of unbounded in-memory map
description:
Currently `m.metas` holds *all* cloud chunk metadata in memory for the lifetime of the manager. At ~282 bytes/entry this is negligible for most deployments, but at high ingest rates with long retention it grows linearly:

- 10 GB/day × 1 year ≈ 58K chunks → ~16 MB (fine)
- 100 GB/day × 1 year ≈ 584K chunks → ~157 MB (noticeable)
- 1 TB/day × 1 year ≈ 5.8M chunks → ~1.5 GB (problematic)

Since gastrolog-8t1yz added a local B+ tree index (`cloud.idx`) that persists all cloud chunk metadata on disk, we can now serve metadata lookups from the index on demand instead of preloading everything.

**Proposed approach**: Replace the unbounded `map[ChunkID]*chunkMeta` for cloud-backed chunks with a fixed-size LRU cache. On cache miss, fall back to a B+ tree `FindGE` lookup. Local (non-cloud) chunks remain in the map unconditionally since they're bounded by disk.

Affected call sites: query planning (time-range filtering over chunk metadata), ListChunks, Delete, ScanAttrs. Each would need a cache-aware lookup path.

title: Replace batched ForwardRecords with client-streaming RPC
description:
The record forwarder accumulates records into a batch slice (up to 100), then sends them in a single unary ForwardRecords RPC. This is a buffered batch collection pattern — records sit in memory waiting for the batch to fill or the flush timer to fire.

A client-streaming RPC would let records flow through as they arrive: no batch slice, no batch size to tune, no flush interval. The channel acts as the buffer, the stream acts as the drain.

Current flow: channel → drain into []forwardEntry → send unary RPC
Proposed flow: channel → send each record on open stream → server acks

This also removes the per-vault grouping in sendBatch (which builds a map[uuid.UUID][]*ExportRecord just to split one batch into N RPCs). With streaming, each record carries its vault ID and the server routes on receive.

title: Persistent system alerts indicator in UI
description:
When something is degraded — a vault fails to initialize, the record forwarder is dropping records, a cluster peer is unreachable — there is no persistent indication in the UI. The user only sees symptoms (missing data, empty vaults) with no way to discover the cause without reading server logs.

**Problem**: A misconfigured vault (missing S3 region) caused the vault to be skipped at startup. The forwarder kept trying to route records to it, filling the buffer and dropping records. The only evidence was server-side log spam. The UI showed nothing.

### Backend

Add a `SystemAlert` concept:
- Components (orchestrator, record forwarder, cluster peer manager) push alerts into a central collector
- Each alert has: ID, severity (warning/error), source component, message, first seen timestamp, last seen timestamp, "still active" flag
- Alerts are keyed so the same issue doesn't create duplicates (e.g. key="vault-init:019cec15-caef")
- Active alerts auto-clear when the condition resolves (vault successfully re-inits on config change, forwarder reconnects)
- Expose via `GetAlerts` RPC or piggyback on the existing `WatchConfig` stream

Initial alert sources:
- Vault failed to initialize (orchestrator, from the new graceful-degradation skip path)
- Record forwarder buffer full / dropping records (sustained, not per-record)
- Record forwarder in backoff (peer unreachable)

### Frontend

- Persistent indicator in the top bar — small dot/badge, amber for warnings, red for errors
- Always visible when there are active alerts, cannot be dismissed
- Clicking opens a panel listing active alerts with severity, component, message, and timestamps
- Alerts disappear from the panel when the backend clears them (condition resolved)

This is the "check engine light" for the cluster. Quiet until needed, impossible to miss when lit.

title: Make index building configurable per vault
description:
Add a `build_indexes` vault parameter (default: true) that lets operators disable index building on local file vaults. Currently `BuildIndexes` is only set to false automatically for cloud-backed vaults, but operators may want to disable it for high-throughput or short-retention vaults where the I/O cost of building indexes isn't worth it.

Would need: a new param in the file vault factory, surfaced in the frontend vault settings form, and wired through to `Vault.BuildIndexes` in the orchestrator.

title: Vault inspector: show cloud-relevant info instead of index status for cloud chunks
description:
The vault inspector currently shows index status (token, attr_key, attr_val, kv_key, etc.) for all chunks regardless of backing. Cloud-backed chunks will always show "missing" for all indexes since indexes aren't built for them — this is correct behavior but looks broken.

For cloud-backed vaults, the inspector should show cloud-relevant information instead:
- Object key / path in the bucket
- Blob size on cloud storage
- Upload timestamp
- Provider + bucket name

The index status section should be hidden entirely for cloud-backed chunks, or replaced with a note explaining that cloud chunks are queried via full scan (no indexes).

title: Coordinate chunk lifecycle with index builds to prevent races
description:
The chunk manager and index manager operate independently with no coordination on chunk lifecycle transitions. This causes a race when a sealed chunk is being uploaded to cloud (or deleted):

1. `postSealWork` schedules both compression and index build concurrently via the scheduler
2. Compression finishes → `uploadToCloud` → `os.RemoveAll(chunkDir)` deletes the directory
3. Index builder runs concurrently or after → recreates the directory → writes `.idx` files
4. Leftover directory with only index files remains — causes `loadExisting` to fail on restart

Currently mitigated for cloud-backed vaults by `BuildIndexes = false` (skips index scheduling entirely). But the general problem remains: there's no gate between "chunk is being removed" and "indexes are being built for this chunk." The `compressWg` exists for compression but nothing equivalent coordinates index builds.

Possible approaches:
- Add an index build WaitGroup similar to `compressWg`, waited on before `os.RemoveAll` in upload/delete paths
- Have the scheduler cancel in-flight index build jobs for a chunk before deleting it (the `RemoveJobsByPrefix` mechanism exists but isn't wired into the upload path)
- Make `postSealWork` sequential: compress first, then index build only if the chunk is still local

title: Merge index building into chunk manager as pluggable builders
description:
The index manager and chunk manager are separate components that operate on the same chunk directories with no lifecycle coordination. This separation causes races (gastrolog-20swg) and forces the orchestrator to coordinate between them.

The chunk manager already builds `_ingest.bt` and `_source.bt` indexes. The remaining search indexes (token, attr_key, attr_val, attr_kv, kv_key, kv_val, kv_kv, json) should also be owned by the chunk manager, giving it full control over the chunk directory lifecycle: seal → compress → build indexes → upload → delete local.

**Approach**: Index builders as plugins injected into the chunk manager, similar to how `RotationPolicy` is injected today. Each builder implements a simple interface (e.g. `BuildIndex(ctx, chunkDir, cursor) error`) and is registered at construction time. The chunk manager calls them as part of its post-seal pipeline, not as a separate externally-scheduled job.

This:
- Eliminates the race between index builds and cloud upload (gastrolog-20swg)
- Removes the need for `BuildIndexes` on the Vault struct — the chunk manager decides based on its own config
- Simplifies the orchestrator — no more `scheduleIndexBuild`, no `IndexManager` in the Vault struct
- Keeps the indexing code decoupled — the chunk manager doesn't import tokenizer/analyzer, it just calls the injected builders

Supersedes gastrolog-20swg (the coordination issue becomes moot once there's only one component).

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: CONSTRAINT: Chunk sealing must remain asynchronous. The chunk manager must open a new active chunk immediately after sealing, before any post-seal work (compression, index building, cloud upload) begins. The write path must never block on post-seal pipeline stages. This is how it works today — the scheduler runs post-seal jobs in background goroutines — and must be preserved in the merged design.

title: Scatterbox ingester: deterministic test signal generator
description:
A test ingester that generates predictable, traceable log lines for verifying pipeline integrity. Unlike chatterbox (random data, random formats), scatterbox produces a deterministic signal that can be verified end-to-end.

### Features

- **Predictable rate**: configurable N records per X seconds, with optional burst mode (Y records in Z bursts)
- **Traceable records**: each log line includes:
  - Monotonic sequence number (per ingester instance)
  - Generation timestamp (wall clock at creation time)
  - Ingester instance ID
  - Burst number (if burst mode)
- **One-shot mode**: generates a single record when the operator clicks a button in the ingester settings UI — useful for debugging routing, forwarding, and indexing one record at a time
- **Verification**: with the sequence number and generation timestamp in the record body, queries can detect gaps, reordering, duplicates, and latency (generation time vs IngestTS vs WriteTS)

### Use cases

- Detect record drops in the forwarder pipeline (sequence gaps)
- Measure ingest latency (generation TS vs WriteTS)
- Verify histogram accuracy (known rate → expected even distribution)
- Debug chunk rotation timing (sequence numbers map to exact records)
- Test cross-node forwarding integrity

### Implementation

Follow the chatterbox pattern: ingester factory in `internal/ingester/scatterbox/`, params for rate/burst/one-shot, registered in `app.go` factories. JSON body with structured fields for easy querying.

title: Histogram shows wrong distribution for cloud-backed chunks
description:
Cloud-backed chunks have no indexes (indexes aren't built for chunks that get uploaded to S3). The histogram fast path uses `findIngestPos` to binary search for record positions at bucket boundaries. For cloud chunks this always returns `(0, false)`, but the code discards the `ok` value and uses `startPos = 0`.

Result: all records from a cloud chunk are dumped into the **last** overlapping histogram bucket (where `bEnd >= IngestEnd` causes `endPos = RecordCount`). Every other bucket gets 0. This creates dramatic spikes of ~17K records alternating with empty gaps — the total count is correct but the distribution is completely wrong.

Also affects level breakdown: `timechartChunkGroups` skips buckets where `findIngestPos` fails, so cloud chunks never show severity colors.

### Fix

When `findIngestPos` returns `(0, false)`, fall back to **linear interpolation** using chunk metadata. We know `IngestStart`, `IngestEnd`, and `RecordCount` — assume even distribution and pro-rate per bucket. Not sample-accurate, but vastly better than the current spike-and-gap pattern.

Apply to both `timechartChunkByIngestTS` (counts) and `timechartChunkGroups` (level breakdown sampling).

### Files
- `internal/query/histogram.go`: `findIngestPos`, `timechartChunkByIngestTS`, `timechartChunkGroups`

title: Investigate data gaps in high-throughput ingest pipeline
description:
At 1000 records/sec from chatterbox, search results show ~15-20 second gaps where no records exist. The gaps coincide with histogram spikes (all records from a chunk dumped into one bucket). The data genuinely isn't there — not a display issue.

### Likely cause

The chatterbox blocks on `out <- msg` when the ingest channel is full (capacity 1000 = 1 second buffer at 1000 msg/sec). The write loop stalls when it can't acquire `o.mu.Lock()` — contention from retention deletes, config reloads, or other operations holding the orchestrator write lock.

During the stall:
1. Write loop blocks on `o.mu.Lock()`
2. `digestedCh` fills (capacity 1000)
3. Digest loop blocks on `digestedCh <-`
4. `ingestCh` fills (capacity 1000)
5. Chatterbox blocks on `out <- msg`
6. Timer stops, records stop being generated
7. When lock releases, everything flushes — burst of records with timestamps clustered together

### Investigation needed

- Profile lock contention on `o.mu` under high ingest + aggressive retention
- Check if retention `Delete` → `postSealWg.Wait()` is causing long lock holds
- Consider whether the write loop should use `o.mu.RLock()` instead of `o.mu.Lock()`
- Consider larger channel buffers or a non-blocking ingest path
- The scatterbox ingester (gastrolog-3jbov) would make this trivially reproducible and verifiable

title: Cloud chunk search returns incomplete results (gaps in data)
description:
Searching cloud-backed chunks returns incomplete results — scatterbox sequence gaps of ~20K records (~25 seconds) appear consistently. The data IS in S3 (verified via MinIO), and the first search after restart returns correct results. Subsequent searches show gaps.

### Confirmed facts
- Local vaults produce correct, gapless search results
- Cloud vaults produce search results with ~25-second gaps matching chunk rotation interval
- The data is NOT lost — MinIO shows all GLCB blobs with correct sizes
- First search after restart works (chunks still local, not yet uploaded)
- Subsequent searches gap (chunks now in cloud)
- No forwarder drop logs, no alerts
- Scatterbox proves: monotonic sequence gaps match the search result gaps exactly

### Suspected areas
- Cloud cursor (GLCB download + seekable zstd) might not return all records
- Search's TS-ordered scanner fallback (buildTSOrderedScanner) for chunks without indexes
- K-way merge or chunk ordering when IngestTS differs from WriteTS across chunks
- selectChunks filtering based on potentially wrong IngestStart/IngestEnd metadata

### What's needed
- Add instrumentation to searchChunkWithRef: log chunk ID, record count from cursor, record count after filtering, for every cloud chunk
- Verify GLCB round-trip: write N records → upload → download → read → verify count matches
- Compare cursor record count with chunk metadata RecordCount

### Related
- gastrolog-5mkc4 (histogram, partially fixed with interpolation)
- Fixes already applied: reorderByTSWithBounds, histogram interpolation

title: Virtual scroll list shows gaps in records that don't exist in the data
description:
The VirtualLogList component shows visual gaps (missing records) when displaying large search results, especially from cloud-backed chunks. Export proves the data is complete — 222K records with zero sequence gaps. The gaps are purely in the rendering.

The gaps coincide with chunk boundaries. When scrolling through results that span multiple chunks (especially cloud chunks that take longer to fetch), the virtual scroll appears to skip blocks of records. This was initially misdiagnosed as a backend search issue.

Verified with scatterbox: monotonic sequence in the exported CSV is continuous, but the UI shows jumps of 7-20K sequence numbers.

### Investigation
- Check if VirtualLogList correctly handles rapid appends from streaming search responses
- Check if the row height estimation causes miscalculation when chunks return at different rates
- Check if the scroll position jumps when new data arrives from a slow cloud chunk download
- The virtual scroll uses @tanstack/virtual — check if the virtualizer is being notified of all items

title: Histogram interpolation for cloud chunks creates phantom records outside visible data
description:
The histogram interpolation for cloud chunks (added in gastrolog-5mkc4 fix) spreads records evenly across the chunk's IngestStart→IngestEnd range. But when retention has deleted older chunks, the interpolation still shows small bars in time ranges where no data exists anymore.

The fix: clamp the interpolation to the intersection of the chunk's time range and the query's time bounds. Records should only appear in buckets where the chunk actually overlaps the query window.

### File
- `internal/query/histogram.go`: `timechartChunkInterpolated`
