title: Histogram: visually distinguish interpolated (cloud) vs exact (local) record counts
description:
Cloud-backed chunks without indexes use linear interpolation for histogram bucket counts. Local chunks with indexes use exact binary search counts. The user can't tell which bars are approximate and which are precise.

### Design
- Bars with interpolated data should have a diagonal hatch pattern overlay
- When a bucket has BOTH interpolated and exact counts, the bar should be split: solid for exact, hatched for interpolated
- This requires the backend to send per-bucket exact vs interpolated counts separately

### Backend
- Add `interpolated_count` to `HistogramBucket` proto (alongside existing `count`)
- `timechartChunkInterpolated` adds to `interpolated_count` instead of `count`
- `timechartChunkByIndex` adds to `count` (exact)
- Both are summed for the total bar height

### Frontend
- Render `count` as solid fill (existing behavior)
- Render `interpolated_count` as hatched overlay on top (or behind)
- CSS/SVG diagonal line pattern for the hatched portion
- Tooltip shows both: "138 exact + 42 interpolated" or similar

title: Histogram: visually indicate cloud data presence (design needed)
description:
The has_cloud_data boolean flag exists in the HistogramBucket proto (added and reverted in gastrolog-22tmb). The backend correctly marks buckets that have cloud chunk coverage. The frontend visualization needs proper design before shipping.

The first attempt (small hatched bars) looked bad — barely visible dashed lines disconnected from the real bars. Needs a design that:
- Is subtle but noticeable
- Integrates visually with the existing bar chart aesthetic
- Works for both pure-cloud buckets and mixed (local + cloud) buckets
- Doesn't mislead about counts (cloud counts are unknown)

Backend work is done (reverted but code is in git history). Frontend needs design iteration.

title: Comprehensive fuzz testing across all binary parsers, codecs, and input handlers
description:
Add Go fuzz tests for all components that parse, decode, or process untrusted/variable input.

## Targets

### Binary format decoders (crash/panic resistance)
- GLCB reader: malformed headers, truncated sections, bad TOC
- idx.log: EncodeIdxEntry/DecodeIdxEntry round-trip + malformed input
- TS index: FindStartPosition with random data slices
- Format header: Decode with random bytes
- Attribute encoding: Encode/Decode with random key-value maps
- String dictionary: Add/Get round-trip with random strings

### Data structures (invariant violations)
- B+ tree: random insert/delete/lookup/scan sequences, verify ordering and count invariants
- Cloud index codec: encode/decode round-trip with random chunkMeta values

### Parsers (crash + correctness)
- Query parser (querylang): random query strings
- Tokenizer: random byte sequences
- Chunk ID: ParseChunkID with random strings
- Syslog parser: random syslog-like input

### Network input (if applicable)
- Record frame decoding (decodeFrame): random byte slices
- Dict-encoded attribute decoding: random byte slices

title: Security review: authentication, authorization, and input validation across backend
description:
Thorough security audit of the entire backend. Not a feature — a review to identify and fix vulnerabilities.

## Scope

### Authentication
- Token/session validation: are all RPC endpoints properly gated?
- Are there endpoints that should require auth but don't?
- Token storage and handling: timing-safe comparisons, secure defaults
- TLS certificate validation paths

### Authorization
- Per-vault access control: can users access vaults they shouldn't?
- Admin-only operations: are they properly restricted?
- Config mutations: who can modify cluster config via Raft?
- Are there privilege escalation paths?

### Input validation
- Query injection: can crafted query strings cause unintended behavior?
- Path traversal: vault dirs, chunk dirs, blob keys — any escape?
- Proto field validation: oversized messages, negative counts, zero-length arrays
- S3/blob key injection via user-controlled vault/chunk names

### Network exposure
- CORS policy: is it too permissive?
- gRPC/Connect metadata: anything sensitive leaking in headers?
- Error messages: do they expose internal paths or stack traces?
- Rate limiting: any missing?

### Data handling
- Secrets in logs: are auth tokens, passwords, or keys ever logged?
- Temp file handling: are cloud blob downloads cleaned up on all paths?
- File permissions: are created files/dirs appropriately restricted?

title: CLI query command with agent-friendly output formats
description:
Add a `gastrolog query` CLI command that executes queries against a running server and outputs results to stdout. Connection parameters (unix socket, addr, token) live in the parent Cobra command, not here.

## Requirements

### Core
- Accept query expression as positional arg
- Support time range flags: --start, --end, --last (e.g. --last 5m)
- Paginate through full result set (resume tokens), stream to stdout
- Exit code: 0 = results found, 1 = no results, 2 = error

### Output formats (--format flag)
- `text` (default for TTY): human-readable, one record per line, colored timestamps + severity
- `json` (default for non-TTY): one JSON object per line (JSONL/NDJSON), full record with all fields — ideal for piping to jq
- `csv`: header row + one record per row
- `raw`: just the raw log body, one per line — for grep/awk pipelines
- `table`: columnar output for timechart/stats results

### Agent-friendly design
- JSONL by default when stdout is not a TTY (detect with os.IsTerminal)
- Structured error output to stderr (never mix errors into stdout data stream)
- --limit flag to cap results (agents need bounded output)
- --fields flag to select which fields appear in JSON/CSV output
- --count flag: just print the count, don't stream records
- --explain flag: print the query plan instead of executing

title: MCP server interface for AI agent integration
description:
Implement a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that exposes GastroLog capabilities as tools for AI agents (Claude, Cursor, etc.).

## Potential tools

### Query
- `search`: execute a query, return structured results (JSONL)
- `explain`: return query execution plan
- `count`: return record count for a query
- `get_fields`: list available fields/attributes across vaults

### Observability
- `get_vaults`: list vaults with record counts, time ranges, storage info
- `get_alerts`: list active system alerts
- `get_stats`: cluster health, ingestion rates, storage usage

### Configuration (read-only initially)
- `get_config`: current server/vault/route configuration
- `list_ingesters`: running ingesters with status

## Design considerations
- Transport: stdio (for local agent use) vs SSE (for remote)
- Connection to GastroLog server via unix socket (same as CLI)
- Tool responses should be concise — agents have context limits
- Pagination: tools should support cursor-based pagination for large result sets
- Consider which tools are read-only vs mutating (start with read-only)

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: MCP server should be mounted as an additional HTTP endpoint on the existing Connect RPC server — not a separate process. The SSE transport is just HTTP, so it sits alongside the existing RPC handlers on the same port. No unix socket plumbing needed; it's another route on the same mux.

title: Memory leak: mmap'd TS index cache files never unmapped
description:
loadCloudTSIndex calls syscall.Mmap on every access to a cloud chunk's TS index cache file and never calls syscall.Munmap. Each query creates new mappings that the Go GC cannot release. With repeated queries across cloud chunks, RSS grows without bound.

Observed: node-1 at 13.3 GB RSS with only 136 MB Go heap in use. The remaining ~13 GB is leaked mmap'd pages.

Root cause: mmapFile() in manager.go creates a new mapping per call. The returned []byte slice is used for binary search then discarded — but mmap'd memory is not garbage collected. It requires explicit syscall.Munmap.

Fix: replace mmap with pread-based binary search (same pattern as tsidx.SearchIngestFile). O(log n) reads with a 12-byte buffer, no mapping, no leak.

title: Globe visualizer for geo-IP log data
description: Add a 3D globe visualization using echarts-gl that plots log sources by geographic location. The geo-IP data is already available via MaxMind integration — this adds an alternative to the existing flat map view. Use the echarts-gl globe component with atmosphere effect, Lambert shading, and scatter3D or lines3D series for plotting source locations.

title: Use protocol-provided timestamps as IngestTS where trustworthy
description:
Several ingesters receive timestamps from the source protocol that represent when the event was captured by an upstream logging system, but discard them in favor of time.Now(). These should be used as IngestTS instead:

- **kafka** — use record timestamp when broker is configured for LogAppendTime. Add a config option to control this (default: use broker timestamp).
- **fluentfwd** — Fluentd's per-event timestamp represents when Fluentd processed the event. Use as IngestTS instead of time.Now().
- **OTLP** — use ObservedTimestamp (when the collector saw it) as IngestTS. Currently only the log record timestamp is used (as SourceTS).

Docker and self ingesters already do this correctly. Syslog/RELP timestamps are source-generated (SourceTS, not IngestTS). MQTT/HTTP/tail have no protocol-level timestamp.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: Update help pages for each affected ingester to document which timestamp field is used as IngestTS and why. Users need to understand that IngestTS may reflect upstream system timing rather than GastroLog arrival time.

title: Search resets to 0 rows after scrolling ~7-8K records
description:
Scrolling through a last=5m search result, after approximately 7-8000 records the histogram disappears and the UI shows '0 rows in 376ms'. The search appears to re-execute or reset, losing all loaded results.

Possible causes:
- React Query refetch due to stale time causing the query to re-execute with a shifted time window
- Resume token invalidation from chunk rotation during scroll
- The frozen time bounds in the resume token not being preserved across page fetches

title: Infinite scroll runs autonomously without user interaction
description:
After switching away from the GastroLog tab and returning, the search results scroll on their own without any user input. Accumulated ~30000 records before being manually stopped.

Likely cause: the IntersectionObserver for infinite scroll sentinel fires deferred callbacks when the tab regains visibility, triggering a cascade of page loads. The scroll position changes from new content, which keeps the sentinel in view, creating a feedback loop.

title: Help dialog unusable while system status heartbeat is polling
description: Cannot select text or interact with the help dialog while the system status heartbeat is polling in the background. The heartbeat response likely invalidates queries or triggers re-renders that steal focus or cause layout shifts, making the help dialog unresponsive to interaction.

title: Replace system status polling with server-push stream
description:
The cluster status, health, and route stats hooks poll on 3-5 second intervals. This should be a server-push stream like WatchConfig — the server knows when stats change and can push updates immediately. Benefits: lower latency for stat updates, no wasted requests when nothing changed, fewer background fetches causing re-render pressure.

Hooks to convert:
- useClusterStatus (5s poll) → server stream
- useHealth (5s poll) → server stream  
- useRouteStats (3s poll) → server stream

Model after the existing WatchConfig pattern (server/query.go WatchConfig RPC + useWatchConfig hook).

title: Allow creating vaults, ingesters, and routes in a disabled state
description:
Currently all entities start enabled immediately on creation. There's no way to create one disabled, configure it fully, then enable it. This is problematic when you want to set up an ingester with all its parameters before it starts accepting traffic, or configure a route's filters before records start flowing through it.

Add an 'enabled' toggle to the create forms for vaults, ingesters, and routes, defaulting to enabled (current behavior) but allowing creation in a disabled state.

title: Move Enabled toggle to top of route settings form
description: The Enabled toggle is at the top of vault and ingester settings forms but at the bottom of the route settings form. Move it to the top for consistency.

title: Add Playwright end-to-end tests for the frontend
description:
The frontend has unit tests (702 via Bun) but no end-to-end browser tests. Playwright would cover real user flows: searching, opening dialogs, navigating help, configuring settings, inspector interactions, and verifying that push updates (WatchConfig, WatchSystemStatus) work in a real browser.

Key flows to cover:
- Search: enter query, see results, infinite scroll, histogram
- Help dialog: open, navigate topics, search, text selection (regression for gastrolog-3kug5)
- Settings: create/edit/delete vaults, ingesters, routes
- Inspector: vault list, chunk detail, cloud chunk info
- Auth: login, logout, password change
- Dark/light mode toggle
- Multi-node cluster status display

Needs a test harness that starts a gastrolog server (or mocks the Connect RPC layer) before tests run.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: Test harness: Docker Compose with a 3-node cluster. Each node runs chatterbox + memory vault. No external dependencies (no MinIO, no Kafka, no MQTT broker). Playwright connects to node-1's HTTP port and tests real user flows against live data. The compose file can double as a demo environment.

title: Switch Docker base image from scratch to busybox
description: The current Dockerfile uses FROM scratch which prevents running entrypoint scripts, exec-ing into containers, or any shell-based orchestration. Switch to busybox (~4 MB) to enable shell scripts while keeping the image minimal. Add a basic entrypoint script that starts gastrolog, demonstrating the pattern for future cluster bootstrapping logic.

title: Fix resize bar and scrollbar colors not updating on theme switch
description: The panel resize bars and scrollbar thumbs retained their dark-mode colors when switching to light mode. Two root causes: (1) resize bars had no explicit background — they were transparent, relying on a parent background that the browser didn't repaint; (2) scrollbar-color is cached by the browser compositor and doesn't respond to CSS class changes. Fixed by giving resize bars explicit theme-aware backgrounds without transition-colors, and setting scrollbar-color via inline JS on theme change.

title: Expandable card triangle indicator always black regardless of theme
description: The collapse/expand triangle (▶/▼) on expandable cards in the Inspector (vaults, ingesters, etc.) is always rendered in black, ignoring the current theme. In dark mode it's nearly invisible against the dark background. Should use theme-aware text color.

title: Memory vault chunks don't build indexes after sealing
description:
The memory chunk manager doesn't implement ChunkPostSealProcessor, so when a memory vault seals a chunk, no post-seal pipeline runs — no token, attr, or KV indexes are built. This means memory vault queries always do sequential scans on sealed chunks instead of using index acceleration.

The file chunk manager implements PostSealProcess (compress → build indexes → cloud upload). The memory chunk manager needs an equivalent that at least builds indexes via the wired index builders.

title: Enforce cluster-aware routing strategy for all RPCs
description:
GastroLog is fully distributed — any node can serve any request. But nothing enforces this at the code level. New RPCs are frequently implemented with local-only logic, silently returning empty/wrong results when the user is connected to a node that doesn't own the data.

Proposed solution: every RPC handler declares a routing strategy upfront.

Strategies:
- RouteLocal: any node can handle (config from Raft, stats from PeerState)
- RouteTargeted: forward to the node owning the vault
- RouteFanOut: execute on all nodes, merge results
- RouteLeader: Raft leader only (config mutations)

Enforcement:
1. Compile-time test: scan generated Connect interfaces, verify every RPC has a declared strategy. Missing = fail.
2. Multi-node test: run targeted RPCs against a non-owning node, verify correct results.
3. Review agent: .claude/agents/cross-node-review inspects diffs for routing correctness.

Every strategy declaration must be verified against the actual implementation, not guessed from the name.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: Additional requirement: it should be possible for a node to explicitly dictate which node executes a request, with the result routed back to the originating node. This enables transparent forwarding — the receiving node can inspect the request, determine the correct target, forward it, and return the result to the client without the client knowing.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: Future consideration: load-aware routing. For RouteLocal RPCs (any node can handle), the coordinator could route to the least loaded node based on stats from PeerState broadcasts (CPU, queue depth). Not needed for RouteTargeted (must go to the vault owner) or RouteFanOut (must hit all nodes). Low priority — only matters at scale when nodes have uneven load.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
## Test Plan

### 1. Strategy Declaration Coverage (compile-time)

A test in `server/` scans all generated Connect service handler interfaces, extracts every RPC method name, and verifies each one appears in the routing strategy registry. Missing entries fail the test. This catches new RPCs added without a routing declaration — the server literally cannot pass tests without declaring intent.

### 2. Interceptor Unit Tests (`cluster/`)

- **RouteTargeted: local vault** — request for a vault owned by this node passes through to the handler without forwarding.
- **RouteTargeted: remote vault** — request for a vault on another node is forwarded via the cluster client. Verify the response is proxied back to the caller unchanged.
- **RouteTargeted: unknown vault** — request for a nonexistent vault returns NotFound, not a forward attempt.
- **RouteLeader: this node is leader** — request executes locally.
- **RouteLeader: this node is follower** — request is forwarded to the leader. Verify leader address resolution works.
- **RouteLocal** — request always executes locally regardless of vault ownership.
- **RouteFanOut** — request is dispatched to all nodes. Verify the merge callback receives results from every node.
- **Undeclared RPC** — interceptor rejects with Internal error during registration (startup guard).

### 3. Multi-Node Integration Tests (`server/multinode_test.go`)

Use the existing `setupMultiNode(t, ["coord", "data-1", "data-2"], WithoutVault("coord"))` harness:

- **Targeted vault operations from non-owning node**: Call GetIndexes, ListChunks, GetChunk on the coord node for a vault that lives on data-1. Verify correct results (not empty, not error). This is the class of bug that gastrolog-24gsl was created to prevent.
- **Leader forwarding from follower**: Call a config mutation (PutVaultConfig) on a follower. Verify it succeeds (forwarded to leader internally).
- **Fan-out search across nodes**: Run a Search from coord with vaults on data-1 and data-2. Verify results include records from both nodes, merge-sorted correctly.
- **Routing after vault migration**: Move a vault from data-1 to data-2 (config change). Immediately call GetIndexes for that vault from coord. Verify it routes to data-2, not data-1.

### 4. Negative Tests

- **Strategy mismatch detection**: Register a RouteTargeted handler that does NOT call remoteNodeForVault. A lint/analysis test (or the cross-node review agent) flags this as a potential bug.
- **Forwarding loop prevention**: A request forwarded to node B should not be forwarded again by node B. Verify the interceptor marks forwarded requests (e.g., via gRPC metadata) and executes locally on the second hop.

### Coverage Goal

Every RouteTargeted RPC must have at least one multi-node test that calls it from a node that does NOT own the vault. This is the minimum bar — if the test passes, the routing works. If someone adds a new RouteTargeted RPC without a multi-node test, the strategy declaration test (item 1) will fail, forcing them to add one.

title: Activate ForwardRPC auto-routing for RouteTargeted RPCs
description:
The routing interceptor is wired in and enforces strategy declarations, but RouteTargeted RPCs still pass through to handler-level routing (remoteNodeForVault → per-RPC Forward* handlers). The ForwardRPC generic dispatch mechanism exists but failed in real cluster testing — the internal mux dispatch (httptest.NewRecorder + Connect protocol) needs integration testing before activation.

Next steps:
1. Write a multi-node integration test (TestMultiNode_ForwardRPC) that exercises the full path: interceptor → ForwardRPC gRPC stream → internal mux dispatch → Connect handler → response
2. Debug why the dispatch fails (likely Connect protocol requirements beyond Content-Type and Connect-Protocol-Version)
3. Once the test passes, re-enable auto-routing in interceptor.go by uncommenting the RouteTargeted forwarding path
4. Verify all 29 existing multi-node tests still pass
5. Optionally: remove handler-level remoteNodeForVault checks once ForwardRPC is proven reliable

title: Logout should revoke only the current refresh token, not all sessions
description: Logout RPC calls InvalidateTokens + DeleteUserRefreshTokens, nuking every session. Fix: add refresh_token to LogoutRequest, delete only that one token.

title: E2E: Playwright test coverage expansion
description: Epic for tracking all issues related to creating new Playwright E2E tests — covering untested UI flows, cluster behaviors, and edge cases.

title: Cluster join info shows port-only address, breaks cross-host joins
description: The "Cluster Join Info" panel displays the join address as `:4566` (port only, no hostname or IP). The generated join command uses `--join-addr :4566`, which implicitly resolves to localhost. This works in single-host dev setups but fails when the joining node runs on a different host or container. The E2E Docker cluster (separate containers per node) is the ideal environment to validate this — write a Playwright test that attempts to use the displayed join info to add a node, exposing the bug.

title: E2E: Test file uploads and management
description: Add Playwright E2E tests covering file upload flows and file management operations in the UI.

title: E2E: Test certificate management
description: Add Playwright E2E tests covering certificate management flows in the UI.

title: E2E: Test cluster settings tabs (Cluster + Nodes)
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for the Cluster and Nodes settings tabs. The existing settings tests only cover Vaults, Ingesters, Routes, and Filters. Should test cluster info display, node listing, join info panel, and any editable cluster settings.

title: E2E: Test lookup table management
description: Add Playwright E2E tests covering lookup table CRUD operations in the settings UI.

title: E2E: Test user management
description: Add Playwright E2E tests covering user management flows — creating, editing, and deleting users, role assignment, and permissions.

title: E2E: Test explain plan
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for the explain plan feature — verifying that query explanation results are displayed correctly across the cluster.

title: E2E: Strengthen follow mode tests
description: Follow mode has been problematic in the past. The existing tests are too soft — the live record assertion catches failures silently and doesn't hard-assert on records rendering. Strengthen with: hard assertion that log entries actually appear, filtered follow (specific query), follow across multiple vaults, follow with continuous ingestion (may need a scatterbox-style ingester that emits data automatically rather than one-shot), verify record count increments, and test reconnection/resilience.

title: E2E: Test stats bar tooltips
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for the stats bar tooltips in the header. Currently only visibility of the stats ribbon is checked — no hover/tooltip interactions are tested. Should verify tooltips appear on hover for each stat (CPU, memory, etc.) and display correct content.

title: E2E: Test event identity
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for the event identity feature — verifying that event identity information is displayed and functions correctly in the UI.

title: E2E: Test histogram tooltips and time span selection
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for the histogram component. Currently only a soft visibility check exists. Should test: hovering bars to show tooltips with bucket details, drag-selecting a time span to narrow the search range, and verifying the search updates after span selection.

title: E2E: Test settings/inspector cross-navigation via heartbeat and cog icons
description: Add Playwright E2E tests verifying that the heartbeat and cog wheel icons correctly navigate between the Settings and Inspector dialogs. Test jumping from inspector to the corresponding settings tab and vice versa, ensuring each link lands on the correct page/tab.

title: E2E: Test exporting query results
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for exporting search/pipeline query results. Verify the export action triggers a download with correct format and content.

title: E2E: Test pipeline operators
description: Add Playwright E2E tests exercising pipeline query operators (count, top, stats, sort, limit, etc.). The existing pipeline test only verifies the ingestion flow end-to-end but never runs a query with pipe operators. Should test that operator results render correctly (tables, charts) and that chaining multiple operators works.

title: E2E: Test left/right collapsible pane interactions
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for the collapsible left and right panes in the search view. Should test: expanding and collapsing each pane, clicking items within each pane (severity filters, vault selectors, field lists, detail sidebar fields), verifying pane state is preserved across interactions, and testing resize handles if applicable.

title: E2E: Test help links in settings and inspector point to correct pages
description: Add Playwright E2E tests verifying that every help icon (?) in the Settings and Inspector dialogs navigates to the correct help topic. Should iterate through all tabs in both dialogs, click each help link, and assert the help dialog opens to the matching topic heading.

title: E2E: Test query history and saved queries
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for the query history popup and saved queries feature. Test opening history, selecting previous queries, removing entries, clearing all. Test saved query CRUD: save current query with name, load saved query, delete saved query, persistence across reloads.

title: E2E: Test search sidebar filters (severity, time range, vault selector)
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for the search sidebar filter controls. Test severity toggle buttons (individual and combined), time range picker (all presets: 1h, 6h, 24h, 7d, 30d, custom date range with validation), and vault multi-select (toggle vault visibility, verify search respects selection).

title: E2E: Test log entry field interactions (click-to-filter, copy)
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for log entry detail panel field interactions. Test clicking a field value to add it as a query filter, copying individual field values to clipboard, expanding/collapsing large messages, and field type formatting (timestamps, UUIDs, etc.).

title: E2E: Test URL-driven state and deep linking
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for URL state persistence. Verify search query appears in URL params, loading a URL with query params pre-fills and executes the search, browser back/forward preserves state, and shareable URLs work for another session.

title: E2E: Test keyboard shortcuts
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for keyboard shortcuts. Test search focus (Cmd/Ctrl+K), Escape to close all dialogs (settings, inspector, help, preferences), and any other global shortcuts. Verify shortcuts work regardless of current focus state.

title: E2E: Test error states and validation
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for error and edge case states. Test invalid query syntax (parser error display), empty result state, form validation errors in settings (duplicate names, invalid values, required fields), and network error handling.

title: E2E: Test rotation and retention policy editing and validation
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for full rotation and retention policy management. The pipeline test only creates/deletes. Add tests for editing existing policies, field validation, and cascade warnings when deleting a policy in use by a vault.

title: E2E: Test pipeline result visualizations (chart types, table toggle)
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for pipeline result rendering. Test chart/table mode toggle, verify different chart types render (bar, donut, heatmap, time series), table column sorting, and single-value result display.

title: E2E: Test auth flows beyond login (password change, roles, session expiry)
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for advanced auth flows. Test password change dialog with validation, role-based access (admin vs user visibility of settings tabs), session expiration handling and redirect to login.

title: E2E: Test inspector jobs and route stats tabs
description: Add Playwright E2E tests for the inspector Jobs and Route Stats tabs. Verify job listing, job status indicators, job detail expansion. Verify route stats display with per-route throughput metrics across the cluster.

title: E2E: Test cross-node config push propagation
description: Add Playwright E2E tests verifying that config changes made via one node are reflected when viewing through another node. Change a setting (e.g. create a vault) via node-1, then query node-2 and verify the change is visible. Critical for cluster correctness.

title: Logout should revoke only the current refresh token, not all sessions
description:
Currently `Logout` calls `InvalidateTokens` + `DeleteUserRefreshTokens`, which nukes every session for the user across all devices. This is because `LogoutRequest` is empty — the server doesn't know which refresh token to revoke.

Fix: add `refresh_token` field to `LogoutRequest`. The client sends its refresh token, the server deletes just that one via `DeleteRefreshTokenByHash(sha256(token))`. Remove the `InvalidateTokens` call from `Logout`.

Steps:
1. Add `string refresh_token = 1` to `LogoutRequest` in `auth.proto`
2. `buf generate`
3. Update `Logout` handler to delete only the provided token
4. Update frontend to send the refresh token in the logout call
5. Remove the `InvalidateTokens` call (keep it only in password change)
6. Add expired-token cleanup (periodic sweep of tokens past `ExpiresAt`)

title: Tiered Storage
description: Vault as logical container with tier chains. Per-tier primaries, inter-tier record streaming, durability handoff, replication as tier property. See docs/vision-issues.md Epic 1.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: Vault replication question (gastrolog-3d5fy) resolved by the tier chain model — replication is per-tier, not per-vault. Object storage tiers get durability for free. Local tiers replicate via per-tier primary/secondary with sealed chunk copies. Memory tiers optionally mirror writes. See docs/vision.md Tiered Storage section.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: Encryption architectural analysis completed — see docs/vision-issues.md 'Architectural Conflict: Encryption' section. Key findings: volume-level encryption for local tiers (preserves mmap), SSE-KMS for cloud tiers, role-based display masking not field-level encryption, BYOK scoped to cloud tiers. Industry comparison covers Elasticsearch, Splunk, ClickHouse, Loki, Datadog, CockroachDB.

title: Multi-Tenancy

title: Performance Benchmarks

title: Define storage types and areas in proto + config
description:
Define the storage model in proto and config:

1. StorageArea — per-node declaration of locally-attached storage. Has a path, a storage class (numeric — lower is faster), a capacity, and a label. Multiple per node. Example: NVMe at class 1, NAS at class 3.

2. CloudService — cluster-wide definition of a cloud storage endpoint. Provider, bucket, credentials, region, storage class. Multiple can exist. Not tied to any node.

3. Three tier types:
   - Memory tier: RAM-backed. Budget limits RAM usage.
   - Local tier: disk-backed. Has a storage class requirement — matched to StorageAreas on nodes.
   - Cloud tier: references a CloudService for sealed chunks. Has a storage class requirement for the active chunk (local disk for staging). Has a separate storage class requirement for the chunk cache (local disk for query cache).

4. Vault: contains one or more tiers, ordered.

This issue covers the proto messages, Go config types, Raft FSM commands, store interface, and store implementations for StorageArea and CloudService. The tier and vault config entities are separate follow-up issues.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Design discussion (2026-03-22):

CURRENT STATE:
- Vault is a struct with one ChunkManager, one IndexManager, one query Engine
- Vault type (memory/file/cloud) determined by which concrete ChunkManager is passed in
- Each vault has a specific storage directory assigned at creation time by the operator
- Each vault is assigned to a specific node

DECOUPLING CHANGES:

1. Storage configuration becomes per-node, not per-vault
   - Nodes declare what storage they have available on registration/startup
   - Each local storage area has a path, a speed class (free-form label like 'fast', 'standard'), and a capacity
   - Example node config:
     storage:
       - path: /data/nvme, class: fast, capacity: 200GB
       - path: /data/hdd, class: standard, capacity: 4TB
   - Multiple local storage paths per node supported (NVMe + HDD, etc.)
   - Speed class is an operator-assigned label, not a numeric ranking — avoids everyone inventing their own scale
   - Memory budget also per-node (how much RAM available for memory tiers)

2. Cloud storage config is cluster-wide (Raft-replicated)
   - S3 buckets, GCS buckets, credentials are shared config — not node-specific
   - Any node with local disk can be a cloud tier primary (cloud tiers need local disk for active chunks)
   - The S3/GCS bucket is accessible from any node — nothing node-local about cloud credentials

3. Vault creation flow changes
   - Operator no longer picks a type or path — just names the vault and configures the tier chain policy
   - Tier chain references speed classes, not paths: memory → local(fast) → local(standard) → s3(default)
   - The system places tier primaries on nodes that have the required medium/class available
   - If a node doesn't have a matching speed class, it can't be elected primary for that tier

4. Tier primary election is constrained by node capabilities
   - A node without 'fast' local storage can't be primary for a local(fast) tier
   - A node with a full disk shouldn't be elected for any local tier
   - Memory budget exhaustion prevents election for memory tiers

5. Open questions
   - What happens when a tier chain references a speed class that no node has? Fail vault creation? Warning?
   - Should there be a default tier chain so operators don't have to configure one for simple deployments?
   - How does the migration from current vault types work? Existing file vaults become single-tier (local) vaults with the current path as their storage area?

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Design discussion continued (2026-03-22):

STORAGE PRIORITY MODEL (replaces speed class labels):

1. Unified priority system
   - Every storage area has a numeric priority (0 = fastest/most expensive, higher = slower/cheaper)
   - Priority 0 is always memory-backed (RAM). Not declared by operator — implicit on every node
   - All other priorities declared by operator with a label, and either a path+capacity (local) or bucket+credentials (cloud)
   - Cloud storage areas declare which local priority to use for their active chunk buffer
   - Example node storage config:
     priority 0: Memory (implicit)
     priority 1: label=NVMe, path=/data/nvme, capacity=200GB
     priority 3: label=NAS, path=/mnt/nas, capacity=10TB
     priority 5: label=S3 Production, bucket=logs-prod, active-buffer=3
     priority 7: label=Glacier, bucket=logs-archive, active-buffer=3

2. Vault tier chain is just a list of priorities
   - Example: [0, 1, 5] = memory -> NVMe -> S3
   - No distinction between local tier and cloud tier in the chain
   - The storage area definition tells the system whether it is RAM, disk, or object storage
   - Operator can skip priorities (e.g. [0, 1, 5] skips 3)
   - Operator can omit memory tier if desired (start at priority 1)

3. No special tier types
   - S3/GCS/Glacier are not special — they are storage areas with a priority like any other
   - The only thing that differentiates them is the storage area definition (bucket vs path)
   - Cloud tiers need local disk for active chunks — the active-buffer field points to another priority

4. Failure handling
   - Configuration time: fail hard. If vault references a priority that no node has, reject the config
   - Runtime (node failure): stall, don't reroute. The upstream tier holds data per durability handoff protocol. This effectively fails up — data stays on faster storage until the downstream tier is available again. Storage pressure alerts tell the operator to fix the tier chain
   - Permanently missing priority: upstream tier fills up, pressure alerts fire, operator reconfigures. System never silently reroutes to a different priority

5. Why not freeform labels or fixed enum
   - Freeform labels (like CockroachDB attrs): system can't reason about ordering. Can't validate that tier chain priorities are ascending
   - Fixed enum (nvme/ssd/hdd like HDFS): doesn't accommodate SAN, NAS, USB, NVMe-oF, or any future storage medium. A network NVMe-oF array might outperform local SATA SSD — device type != performance
   - Numeric priorities: operator defines the ordering based on their actual hardware. System validates ascending order in tier chains. Labels for human readability in UI

6. Single-node deployments
   - Everything still works. All tier primaries on the same node, no secondaries, no replication for local tiers. The tier chain is an in-process pipe. Cloud tiers still get S3 durability

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Industry research on storage classification and tiered placement (2026-03-22):

INFRASTRUCTURE SYSTEMS:

Kubernetes StorageClasses: Fully operator-defined taxonomy. StorageClass has a name, provisioner, and opaque parameters bag. PVCs reference by name. K8s deliberately avoids modeling storage capabilities — pushes all semantics to CSI drivers. Maximum flexibility, zero cross-provider portability.

Ceph CRUSH maps: Device classes (hdd/ssd/nvme) auto-detected from kernel, with operator override. Custom classes allowed. CRUSH rules select device classes via shadow hierarchies — filters the placement tree by class. Elegant reuse of existing placement algorithm.

HDFS storage policies: Fixed enum of 4 types (RAM_DISK > SSD > DISK > ARCHIVE) with implicit ranking. Policies define placement + fallback chains (what to use when preferred tier is full). Background Mover reconciles actual placement with declared policy. Fallback chain concept is useful for GastroLog.

ZFS: No tiering taxonomy. Devices assigned to architectural roles (data/slog/cache/special). Special vdev steers metadata and small files to fast media via block-size threshold. Not a cache — first-class storage with overflow to regular vdevs.

CockroachDB: Two label systems — locality (hierarchical location) and store attributes (freeform capability tags). Zone configs use +/- prefix constraints. Inheritance hierarchy: cluster > database > table > index > partition. Freeform attrs mean no validation — referencing a nonexistent attr silently under-replicates.

TiKV: Freeform key-value labels on stores. Placement policies in SQL with constraint syntax. Unique feature: dictionary constraints ({+disk=ssd: 1, +disk=nvme: 2}) for per-tier replica counts. Kubernetes-style filter primitives (in/notIn/exists/notExists).

Cloud providers (AWS/GCP/Azure): All converge on small fixed enum of named tiers with performance profiles. Trend toward decoupled provisioning (independently set IOPS and throughput). Users cannot create new types.

DATABASE AND LOG SYSTEMS:

Kafka tiered storage: Two tiers (local/remote), binary. Metadata tracked in internal Kafka topic. Two-retention model: local.retention.ms (how long on fast disk) + retention.ms (how long total). Clean separation. Active segments never tiered.

Cassandra: No tiered storage. All data directories treated as equal peers. Deliberately chose NOT to support heterogeneous storage — data safety argument (tombstone resurrection risk if disks fail independently).

Elasticsearch ILM: Four tiers (hot/warm/cold/frozen) as first-class node roles. Evolved FROM arbitrary labels TO fixed roles because freeform was error-prone. Frozen tier uses searchable snapshots (local cache over S3, no replicas needed — snapshot is source of truth). _tier_preference has fallback chain.

ClickHouse: Three-level hierarchy (disk -> volume -> policy). Dual migration triggers: space pressure (move_factor) + time (TTL MOVE rules). Per-table policies. Most granular model in survey. TTL MOVE can also change compression codec on migration.

Splunk: Raw path-based (homePath/coldPath/thawedPath). Volumes as named paths with size caps. Hot+warm share same path. Frozen is terminal (delete or external archive via script hook). No abstraction for storage type.

Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift: Fully opaque. No operator knobs. BigQuery tiering is purely a billing change (50% discount after 90 days). Redshift RA3 uses local SSD as transparent cache over S3 with temperature-based eviction.

KEY TAKEAWAYS FOR GASTROLOG:

1. Freeform labels are flexible but error-prone (Elasticsearch abandoned them, CockroachDB silently under-replicates)
2. Fixed enums are predictable but don't accommodate diverse hardware
3. Numeric priorities (our chosen model) sit between these — operator defines ordering, system can validate
4. Fallback chains (HDFS) and _tier_preference (Elasticsearch) are important — we handle this via durability handoff stall
5. ClickHouse's dual trigger model (space pressure + TTL) maps to our time-based + size-based transition policies
6. Kafka's metadata-in-own-system approach validates storing chunk metadata in Raft
7. Elasticsearch's searchable snapshots (frozen tier = cache over object store) validates our on-demand promotion design
8. Background reconciliation (HDFS Mover, MinIO ILM) is the common pattern — declare intent, async process migrates

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Cloud storage considerations (2026-03-22):

CLOUD STORAGE IS NOT LIMITLESS — COST CONSTRAINTS:

1. Per-request costs
   - S3: PUT/GET/LIST priced per-request. Thousands of tiny chunks = thousands of LIST calls
   - This is why cloud tiers need their own chunking schedule producing fewer, larger objects
   - Key prefix partitioning needed at scale (S3: ~5,500 PUT/s, ~3,500 GET/s per prefix)

2. Egress costs
   - Downloading data from S3 costs money per GB
   - On-demand promotion (cold -> warm) incurs egress on every fetch
   - Frequent promotion/eviction cycling can negate storage savings
   - Access-based transition policy must account for this: don't demote frequently-queried data

3. Minimum retention charges
   - Glacier: 90-day minimum charge regardless of actual retention
   - Glacier Deep Archive: 180-day minimum
   - Delete early and you still pay for the full minimum period

4. Budget-based policy must include total cost
   - Storage cost (GB-months) + request costs (PUT/GET/LIST) + egress costs (downloads)
   - A vault's $30/month budget is the total, not just storage

LOCAL CACHE FOR CLOUD CHUNKS:

Cloud-stored sealed chunks should be cached locally on-demand to avoid repeated egress costs and latency:

1. Query hits a cloud-stored chunk -> download to local disk -> serve from local -> keep in cache
2. Cache uses the on-demand promotion mechanism (issue 1.20) with a local storage priority for cache location
3. Cache eviction policy: LRU or TTL-based (issue 1.21)
   - LRU: evict least-recently-queried chunks when cache fills up
   - TTL: evict chunks not accessed within N hours/days
   - Hybrid: TTL floor (keep at least 1 hour) + LRU when space pressure hits
4. Cache is purely a performance optimization — the cloud copy is the source of truth
5. Cache hit avoids egress cost entirely. Cache sizing directly impacts cloud cost
6. This is the same pattern as Elasticsearch's frozen tier searchable snapshots and Redshift RA3's managed storage — local SSD as transparent cache over object store
7. Cache should be shared across all vaults using the same cloud priority — not per-vault caches competing for the same disk

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
CONSOLIDATED DESIGN (2026-03-22) — supersedes comments c1 and c2:

STORAGE PRIORITY MODEL:

Every storage area has a numeric priority (0 = fastest, higher = slower/cheaper).

Priority 0 is memory-backed (RAM), implicit on every node. The per-node memory budget constrains how much RAM is available for priority 0 tiers.

All other priorities are declared per-node:
  - Local storage: priority, label, path, capacity
  - Cloud participation: priority, label, active-buffer priority (which local priority to use for unsealed chunks and cache)

Cloud backend definitions (bucket, credentials, region) are cluster-wide config in Raft. Per-node declarations reference these backends and specify local resources for active chunks and caching.

Example node declaration:
  priority 0: Memory (implicit, budget: 4GB)
  priority 1: label=NVMe, path=/data/nvme, capacity=200GB
  priority 3: label=NAS, path=/mnt/nas, capacity=10TB
  priority 5: cloud backend=s3-prod, active-buffer=3, cache=3
  priority 7: cloud backend=glacier-archive, active-buffer=3, cache=3

Example cluster-wide cloud backends:
  s3-prod: bucket=logs-prod, region=eu-west-1, credentials=...
  glacier-archive: bucket=logs-archive, region=eu-west-1, storage-class=GLACIER

VAULT TIER CHAIN:

A list of ascending priorities: [0, 1, 5] = memory -> NVMe -> S3.

No distinction between local and cloud tiers in the chain. The storage area definition determines the implementation. Operator can skip priorities or omit memory.

FAILURE HANDLING:

Config time: fail hard if a referenced priority doesn't exist on any node.
Runtime: stall (upstream tier holds data via durability handoff). Effectively fails up. Storage pressure alerts fire. System never silently reroutes.

SINGLE-NODE:

All tier primaries on same node. No secondaries, no local replication. Cloud tiers still get provider durability. Tier chain is an in-process pipe.

CLOUD STORAGE COSTS AND CACHING:

Budget-based policies must include storage + request + egress costs. Cloud-stored sealed chunks cached locally (LRU/TTL, shared across vaults on same cloud priority) to avoid repeated egress. Cache location uses the active-buffer priority. See comment c4 for details.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Live tier chain reconfiguration (2026-03-22):

Reconfiguration affects the future, not the past.

Adding a tier: new data streams to the inserted priority. Existing data in downstream tiers stays put — no back-migration.

Removing a tier: the removed priority enters wind-down state. No new writes. Existing sealed chunks remain part of the vault — queryable, visible in inspector, counted in storage metrics. Chunks drain forward to the next active priority in the background. Once empty, the tier is fully removed.

Key invariant: orphaned data in a removed tier still belongs to the vault. Never invisible, never silently discarded.

Changing transition policies: new thresholds apply to future evaluations only.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Implementation approach (2026-03-22):

The current Vault struct is just a component tuple (ChunkManager + IndexManager + query Engine) registered with the orchestrator. All lifecycle behavior (seal, compress, rotate, retain) lives in the orchestrator, not the vault.

Step 1: Create a Tier entity
- Extract the current Vault struct into a Tier
- Move lifecycle operations (seal, compress, rotate, retain, schedule) from the orchestrator into the Tier
- Tier owns: ChunkManager, IndexManager, query Engine, rotation policy, retention policy, node assignment, state (active/wind-down/empty)
- The orchestrator calls into Tiers instead of directly manipulating chunk managers

Step 2: Create the new Vault entity
- Vault owns: ID, name, ordered list of Tiers (by priority), transition policies
- Vault manages: inter-tier record streaming, durability handoff, tier chain reconfiguration
- Vault delegates queries to all its Tiers and merges results
- The orchestrator manages Vaults, which manage their Tiers

This is a refactor of existing code first, new functionality second. The Tier does exactly what the current Vault+orchestrator does today. The new Vault adds the tier chain on top.

comments.text: Open question: what happens when destination tier replication is slow/unreachable? Unbounded hold is a resource leak, timeout means potential data loss. See docs/vision-issues.md open question 1.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Implementation context from replication work (gastrolog-18ybo):

Current behavior:
- Sealed-chunk replication runs in post-seal pipeline: seal → compress → index → replicate to secondaries
- Tier 1 primary deletes chunk immediately after transitioning records to Tier 2
- Tier 1 secondaries retain their copies until their own retention sweep expires them (all actions forced to expire on secondaries)
- Tier 2 primary receives records, seals, then replicates to Tier 2 secondaries in its own post-seal pipeline
- During the handoff window: Tier 1 secondaries (RF-1 copies) + Tier 2 primary (1 copy) = RF total, but Tier 2 alone has only 1 copy until replication completes

What this issue needs to add:
- TransitionAck mechanism: after Tier 2 confirms RF-complete replication, it sends an ack back to Tier 1
- Pending handoff state on chunks: Tier 1 marks transitioned chunks as 'awaiting ack' instead of deleting immediately
- Tier 1 retention sweep skips chunks in pending-handoff state
- Timeout policy: if ack doesn't arrive within a configurable window, either hold indefinitely (safe) or expire with a warning (see open question in comment 1)
- The ack could piggyback on the existing ForwardImportRecords response, or be a separate RPC

Key files:
- orchestrator/replication.go — replicateSealedChunk (the completion callback would trigger the ack)
- orchestrator/transition.go — transitionChunk / transitionLocal / transitionRemote (need to track pending handoff instead of immediate delete)
- orchestrator/retention.go — sweep needs to skip pending-handoff chunks
- cluster/chunk_transferrer.go — ReplicateSealedChunk (could return a completion signal)

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
REWORKED: This issue now covers ack gating AND the durability handoff for tier transitions.

Part 1 — Ack gating: Delay the IngestMessage.Ack until RF-1 secondaries confirm receipt (depends on gastrolog-4h7og for the forwarding). Opt-in: only fires for ingesters with non-nil Ack (RELP, HTTP X-Wait-Ack). Fire-and-forget ingesters stay async.

Part 2 — Tier transition handoff: Tier 1 should not delete a transitioned chunk until Tier 2 has RF-complete sealed-chunk replication. TransitionAck mechanism, pending-handoff state on chunks.

Part 3 — Sealed-chunk handoff on secondaries: When sealed-chunk replication delivers a canonical sealed chunk to a secondary, the secondary must discard its active chunk (raw records from fire-and-forget forwarding). Without this, the active chunk is never sealed (rotation disabled on secondaries), never cleaned by retention (only processes sealed chunks), and grows forever. Trigger: ImportToTier on a secondary discards the active chunk before importing.

Key files: lifecycle.go:244 (ack point), transition.go (handoff state), vault_ops.go ImportToTier (active chunk discard).

The current Vault struct (ChunkManager + IndexManager + query.Engine) becomes a per-tier local instantiation managed by the orchestrator, not a field on the Tier config entity. The Tier is logical (config); the chunk manager is physical (node-local).

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text:
Document the interaction between rotation and retention timing:
- Retention only acts on sealed chunks; the active chunk is never touched
- Age is measured from WriteEnd (last record written), not WriteStart
- Effective minimum data lifetime = rotation period + retention age + sweep interval (1 min)
- If retention age < rotation period, the rotation period is the effective floor
- Operators should ensure rotation period ≤ retention age for predictable behavior

title: Node storage area registration (CLI flags + UI add form)
description:
Nodes need to declare their locally-attached storage areas before local tiers can be created. Two paths, no gap between them:

1. CLI: gastrolog server flags to declare storage areas on startup (e.g. --storage /data/nvme:class=1:label=NVMe). Registers via SetNodeStorageConfig on boot.

2. UI: Storage settings tab node section gets an add form for creating/editing storage areas interactively.

Both call SetNodeStorageConfig. Without this, local tiers have nothing to match storage class against — this blocks all tier-related work.
