title: Retention sweep never runs on cross-node secondary tiers
Retention sweep never runs on cross-node secondary tiers
description: applyTierRetention (startup path via AddVault) explicitly skips IsSecondary tiers. handleVaultPut returns immediately after AddVault, so ReloadRetentionPolicies (which correctly handles secondaries) is never reached on first vault creation. Result: secondary nodes never get retention runners — chunks replicated to secondaries accumulate forever. Evidence: primary (node2) has 4 chunks, all three secondaries have 334 each for the same tier.

title: Retention sweep never runs on cross-node secondary tiers
Simplify cluster lifecycle: discovery-based reconciliation
description: Replace fragile event-driven lifecycle management with discovery-based periodic sweeps. The retention bug (gastrolog-200am) proved that multiple code paths maintaining per-instance state maps inevitably diverge. The fix — a single sweep that discovers what exists and reconciles from config — was simpler, more correct, and eliminated an entire class of bugs. Apply the same pattern to rotation policies, filter compilation, replication targets, ingester lifecycle, and vault teardown.

title.new: Simplify cluster lifecycle: discovery-based reconciliation

title: Simplify cluster lifecycle: discovery-based reconciliation
Replace per-tier rotation job lifecycle with discovery-based sweep
description: applyTierRotation and reloadTierRotation are separate paths that manually maintain cron jobs in a map (add/update/remove state machine). Same bug shape as the retention issue (gastrolog-200am). Replace with a single rotationPolicySweepAll that discovers all local primary tiers each tick and reconciles cron jobs from current config.

title: Replace per-tier rotation job lifecycle with discovery-based sweep
Replace multi-path filter recompilation with periodic reconciliation
description: reloadFiltersFromRoutes is called from 3 places (factory init, AddVault, ReloadFilters) with different lock state and error handling. AddVault does a rollback on failure, factory doesn't. Replace with a single idempotent reconcileFilters() that runs periodically or on-demand, always deriving the filter set from current config + registered vaults.

Compute replication targets from config at use time instead of caching
description: tier.SecondaryTargets is populated during config changes (dispatch.go) and read during replication (vault_ops.go). If config changes mid-flight, targets are stale. Instead, compute targets from the current tier config at the start of each replication cycle. Removes the cached field and the dispatch code that maintains it.

Make AddIngester idempotent (stop-old-start-new)
description: The dispatch handler uses a remove-then-add pattern for ingesters (check if running, remove, then add). This is fragile — if remove fails, the add is skipped. Make AddIngester idempotent: if an ingester with the same ID already exists, stop the old one and replace it atomically.

Tier Raft FSM empty on secondary nodes — chunk manifest not propagating
description: The tier Raft ChunkFSM on secondary nodes appears to be empty even when the primary has committed CmdDeleteChunk entries. This prevents both the OnDelete callback and the manifest-based reconciliation from working. The tier Raft groups may not be properly connected between primary and secondary nodes. Needs investigation: check Raft group membership, transport connectivity, and whether CreateGroup on secondaries actually joins the primary's group. Root cause of why Raft-based retention propagation fails.

Define the storage/tier/node relationship for multi-store replication
description: Same-node replication (multiple file stores per tier per node) doesn't map cleanly to the Raft group model. The tier Raft has one member per node, but a node can have N physical replicas. The chunk manifest is tier-scoped but storage is instance-scoped. This creates ambiguity in delete propagation, reconciliation, and partial replication failure handling. Need to decide: should each store be its own Raft member? Should the manifest track storage IDs? Or should the Raft stay tier-scoped with a separate per-store reconciliation layer?

title.new: Define the storage/tier/node relationship for multi-store replication

description.new: Same-node replication (multiple file stores per tier per node) doesn't map cleanly to the Raft group model. Should the manifest track storage IDs? Or should the Raft stay tier-scoped with a separate per-store reconciliation layer?

title: Define the storage/tier/node relationship for multi-store replication
Enforce 1:1:1 tier/node/storage relationship
description: Same-node multi-store replication breaks the Raft model: one Raft member per node but N physical replicas. The manifest, OnDelete callback, and reconciliation all assume 1:1 mapping. Enforce that a tier can use at most one storage instance per node. If a node has multiple stores with the same class, placement picks one. Users wanting RF on the same server should run two node processes. This simplifies the entire replication/retention/Raft stack and makes the primary election cleanly map to the primary replica.

title.new: Enforce 1:1:1 tier/node/storage relationship

title: Enforce 1:1:1 tier/node/storage relationship
Enforce 1:1:1 tier/node/storage relationship
description:
Replace config-driven primary/secondary tier assignment with Raft leader election.

Current model: tier config specifies PrimaryNodeID and SecondaryNodeIDs via storage placements. Same-node multi-store replication creates N TierInstances per node, breaking the 1:1 Raft member mapping. The manifest, OnDelete callback, and reconciliation all assume 1:1 but reality is 1:N.

New model:
- Enforce 1 store per tier per node (1:1:1 tier/node/storage)
- Raft leader = tier leader. No separate primary assignment.
- Leader's cm.List() (on-disk reality) is the source of truth
- Leader announces chunk lifecycle to tier Raft → becomes the manifest
- Followers reconcile local disk against the manifest
- Drop primary/secondary nomenclature → leader/follower (Raft's own terms)
- Users wanting RF on same physical machine run two node processes

This collapses the placement/primary-assignment config layer, eliminates the IsSecondary/PrimaryNodeID fields, and makes the tier Raft the single authority for both leadership and chunk lifecycle.

Multi-node-per-machine deployment works via the existing multiraft transport (shared port). Quorum risks of co-located members are the user's choice.

comments.author: kluzz@radical.org
comments.text: Cloud tiers: the 1:1:1 model applies — one store per node for staging + future cache. The blob store is outside Raft scope. CloudBacked chunks in the manifest that aren't on local disk are cache misses, not replication gaps. Caching is not fully implemented yet — preserve current cloud tier behavior, deal with cache reconciliation when caching is built.

title: Enforce 1:1:1 tier/node/storage in config validation
description: Reject or silently pick one store when a tier placement assigns multiple stores on the same node. This is the prerequisite for all other changes — the constraint must hold before Raft leadership can replace config-driven primary assignment.

title.new: Enforce 1:1:1 tier/node/storage in config validation

Remove same-node multi-store TierInstance creation
description: The buildTierInstances loop in reconfig_vaults.go creates N secondary TierInstances per node (one per local storage target). With 1:1:1 enforced, simplify to at most one TierInstance per tier per node. Remove wireOnDeleteCallback chaining, the buildTierInstanceForStorage path for same-node secondaries, and related test helpers.

Replace PrimaryNodeID with Raft leader election
description: Remove the config-driven PrimaryNodeID field from TierInstance and TierConfig placement. The tier Raft leader IS the tier leader. Retention, rotation, replication, and forwarding should check Raft leadership state instead of a static config field. This eliminates the IsSecondary field — a node is a follower if it's not the Raft leader.

Wire retention and replication to Raft leadership
description: With 1:1:1 and Raft leader = tier leader: the leader runs retention sweeps and applies CmdDeleteChunk to the tier Raft. Followers reconcile local disk against the FSM manifest. OnDelete callback fires on manifest updates for immediate propagation. Replication targets derived from Raft group membership instead of config placements.

Search hits deleted chunk during retention race
description: When retention deletes a chunk mid-query, the search path errors with 'vault not found' when trying to read an anchor position from the deleted chunk. The query reads the chunk list, retention deletes a chunk, then the query tries to open a cursor on the deleted chunk. Should handle missing chunks gracefully (skip, don't error). More visible now that retention runs faster with the discovery-based sweep.

Inspector record count is best-effort, not authoritative
description: The vault header record count sums chunk.recordCount from the deduped ListChunks response (fan-out to all nodes). The count varies depending on replication state, retention timing, and which nodes responded. Should use the tier leader's chunk list as the authoritative source instead of a union of all replicas.

title.new: Inspector record count is best-effort, not authoritative

Restructure vault/tier data paths to use IDs and match entity hierarchy
description:
Vault and tier data directories use a mix of entity names and inconsistent scoping:

Current state:
- Vault dir defaults to entity name: vaults/<vaultName>/ — renaming a vault would require moving directories
- File tiers scope by tier ID inside the storage area: <area_path>/<tier_id>/ — correct ID usage but disconnected from vault hierarchy
- Cloud tiers use the raw storage area path with no scoping — two cloud tiers with the same active chunk class share a directory, stomping on each other's cloud.idx, lock file, and chunk data

Target state:
- All paths use entity IDs, never names
- Directory structure mirrors entity relationships: <data_dir>/vaults/<vault_id>/tiers/<tier_id>/
- Renames are free (no filesystem operations needed)
- Each tier gets its own isolated directory regardless of type

Changes needed:
- resolveVaultDir: use vault ID instead of vault name
- buildTierParams: scope ALL tier types (file, cloud, JSONL) by tier ID under the vault directory
- Migration: detect old-style name-based paths and symlink or move to new ID-based paths
- Update any path assumptions in loadExisting, loadCloudChunks, etc.

Note: this is a breaking change for existing data directories. Needs a migration strategy (symlinks, move-on-startup, or dual-path resolution).

title: Restructure vault/tier data paths to use IDs and match entity hierarchy
Replace custom chunk ID format with UUIDs
description: Now that all directory paths use UUIDs (vault IDs, tier IDs), chunk IDs should follow suit. The current base32-encoded custom format (06ejv...) is harder to read, grep, and correlate with other entity IDs. Switch to standard UUIDv7 for chunk IDs. Breaking change for existing data — acceptable since no production deployments exist.

Bootstrap script for automated cluster setup
description: Create scripts/bootstrap.sh that fully automates cluster setup: starts N nodes, registers admin user, creates file storage on each node, creates rotation/retention policies, filter, vault with 2 tiers, route, and disabled ingesters. Uses the gastrolog CLI for all operations. Update justfile cluster recipes to use it.

Show retention status on chunks in inspector
description: Chunks being processed by retention should show a visual indicator in the inspector. Attempted implementation using tier Raft FSM CmdRetentionPending + local inflight map, but the flag never appeared in the UI — likely because the retention processing completes between inspector polls. Needs investigation: either the Raft apply for the pending flag isn't reaching the inspector's ListChunks path, or the dedup logic discards the flagged copy. Deferred.
